projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of an existing placer mine on 8 claims on lower Hunker Creek within the Klondike River Watershed, approximately 11 km east of Dawson City. The Project is located within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory and falls within Land Management Unit 12: Tr’ondëk täk’it (Klondike Valley) of the Dawson Planning Region. Access to the area is via the Klondike Highway from Dawson City and will utilize new and existing access. Operations are proposed from March 1 through October 31, annually, for 10 years.

records.title: Placer Mine - Hunker Creek and Klondike River

projectScope.summary: The purpose of the project is to repair a portion of the Livingstone Winter Trail access.  Approximately 100 m of the Trail has been washed out just before the Teslin River Crossing. The Proponent is proposing to divert waterflow back into the existing creek bed and repair the washed-out section with fill sourced onsite. Equipment will be mobilized to site Winter 2025, with repair work carried out Summer 2025. The Project is located approximately 60 km from Whitehorse and falls within the territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta’an Kwäch’än Council.

projectScope.activities:
Access

A-Star for transportation of personnel and gear (via Dawson City)
EC-120 for transportation for fry release (from Whitehorse via Dawson City)

Open areas and gravel bars will be used for landing near active working sites, vegetation clearing is avoided

Flight paths will avoid Bear Cave Mountain grizzly bear denning areas
Any wildlife observed during flight operations will be avoided to the fullest extent possible


Safety

A comprehensive safety plan will be in place for:

Weather conditions
Wildlife interactions
Swift water and flooding conditions
Work with and around helicopters

Field activities suspended if safety becomes a concern
Experienced personnel

Camp Use and Crew

The Project will make use of an existing Fisheries and Oceans Canada (DFO) field camp
Up to 3 people
Bear safety measures are in place 

Bear fencing 
Bear-proof food and waste storage

Waste will be incinerated on site or removed
Use of a pit latrine (outhouse) for human waste


Fall Field Work - Between September 25 and October 25

Site Selection and Preparation

Selection of up to 12 gravel-rich sites
Fine sediment removal to mimic natural redds
Construction of mounded gravel features

Use of shovels, rakes and other hand tools

Conducting stream flow and sediment assessments 
Baseline data collection (e.g. point velocity, Wolman pebble count, temperature logging, etc.)

Chum Capture and Fertilization

Up to 20 females and 20 males 

Captured using dipnets or targeted angling

Use of fish holding tubes/bags

On-site fertilization

In small batches to minimize fish handling time
Up to 12 hours (in 2 to 3-hour batches)
River water used to harden the fertilized eggs (at least 15 minutes)

Up to 0.25 m3/day

Whitlock-Vibert Boxes 

Small perforated plastic box (up to 300 eggs per box)

Closed Mesh Bags

Spring Field Work - Between March 25 to April 30


Fry flown back to site for direct release
Instream incubation egg survival survey

Manual excavation of bed material and artificial redds to retrieve incubation equipment
projectScope.summary:
The Project is a research and restoration-focused initiative to evaluate the feasibility of using instream egg incubation and portable egg eyeing stations to support the restoration of chum salmon spawning in the Fishing Branch River and its tributaries in the Fishing Branch River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 130 km south of Old Crow and 100 km east of Eagle Plains. The Project will be accessed by helicopter. 
The Project is within Vuntut Gwitchin First Nation Territory and overlaps with Settlement Land parcels VGNF S-3A1 and R5a. The Project falls within the North Yukon Planning Region Land Management Unit: Ni'iinlii'njik (Fishing Branch) SMA and Subunit: Ni'iinlii'njik Protected Area. The Project also overlaps Ni'iinlii Njik (Fishing Branch) Territorial Park (Ecological Reserve and Wilderness Preserve). The Project activities are proposed to occur over a 10 day period in late September to late October, followed by a four day period in late March 25 to late April 30 the following year.  These activities can occur sometime between 2026 and 2029.

records.title: Chum Salmon Restoration Trial - Fishing Branch River

projectScope.summary: The Project is the restoration of the S.S. Klondike vessel, construction of an access boardwalk to the Atlin Barge, and removal of contaminated materials from the S.S. Klondike Historical Site. The Project is on the Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta’an Kwäch’än Council and within the City of Whitehorse. All activities will take place within the footprint of the S.S. Klondike Historical Site. Project activities are proposed to commence in the spring of 2025 and continue for 5 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the upgrading of five existing hydrometric cableway stations at different sites to ensure safe and accurate data collection. The ongoing operation of the hydrometric cableway stations is indefinite and does not form part of this assessment. Reconstruction activities are expected to take up to 10 days per site, between April and October, over the next five years.
Except for access clearing and development, reconstruction activities will occur within the limits of Environment and Climate Change Canada (ECCC) administrative reserves. Further location details of the five project sites are as follows:

The Big Creek Near the Mouth (09AH003) site is located approximately 65 km northwest of Carmacks, within the Territory of Selkirk First Nation (SFN) and the asserted Territory of White River First Nation. The ECCC reserve does not overlap Settlement Land; however, helipad and trail clearing activities on the left bank will occur on parcel SFN R-6A and helicopter access clearing on the right bank will occur on parcel SFN R-40B.
The Duke River Near the Mouth (09CA004) site is located approximately 8 km west of Burwash Landing, within the Territories of Kluane First Nation (KFN) and White River First Nation. All project activities will occur on Settlement Land parcel KFN R-1A.
The Nisling River Below Onion Creek (09CA006) site is located approximately 96 km southeast of Beaver Creek, within the Territory of Selkirk First Nation and the asserted Territory of White River First Nation. All project activities on the left bank will occur on Settlement Land parcel KFN R-25B.
The Nordenskiold River Below Rowlinson Creek (09AH004) site is located approximately 4 km south of Carmacks, within the Territory of Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation and the asserted Territory of White River First Nation. No project activities will occur on Settlement Land.
The Takhanne River at km 167 Haines Highway (08AC001) site is located approximately 78 km south of Haines Junction, within the Territory of Champagne and Aishihik First Nations (CAFN). No project activities will occur on Settlement Land; however, the ECCC reserve is adjacent to parcel CAFN S-361B1.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is to conduct bank reinforcement and culvert replacement and upgrade work at three culverts at km 368.5, 369.2, and 381.3 along the North Klondike Highway. The Project is located approximately 15 km north of Carmacks, within the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation (LSCFN) Territory. Project activities will occur within 50 m of the highway centreline and are expected to occur over one construction season, between the months of May and October, within the next five years. 
The Project is located near LSCFN Settlement Land; however, the proposed activities will not occur on Settlement Land and will not impact access to Settlement Land parcels or private property.

projectScope.summary: Liard First Nation (LFN) is proposing land clearing, soil testing, access development and building construction on three lots (35, 27-1 and 61) within the municipal boundaries of Watson Lake. The Project is located within Kaska Territory (Liard First Nation and Ross River Dena Council as defined by YESAA), on LFN Interim Protected Land parcel LFN C-55. The development of these lots is considered permanent; site preparations and construction activities are anticipated to begin as soon as permits are obtained and to be completed over 20 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 5 claims, along Younger Creek in the Fortymile River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 107 km west of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 21: Wëdzey Tąy (Fortymile Caribou Corridor) of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory. The Project will be accessed using an existing road which connects to the Top of the World Highway. Project activities will occur between June 1 – September 30, annually, for up to 10 years.

Construction activities are anticipated to start in October 2026 and will take up to three years to complete.

projectScope.summary: The Project involves timber harvesting and fuel abatement, which will provide fuel wood, sawlogs and biomass for local markets and reduce wildfire risk to the local community through fuel reduction and stand conversion. The Project partially implements the Teslin North and Strawberry Creek Timber Harvest Plan, opening a western portion of the Teslin North Management Area – B located 1 km north of the Village of Teslin. The area (131.9 ha) is divided into three parts: one personal-use fuelwood area (9.3 ha) and two gated timber harvest areas (57.8 ha and 64.8 ha). The Project is within the Traditional Territory of the Teslin Tlingit Council and is planned to operate for 20 years.

projectScope.summary:
Project Scope was updated July 22, 2025 - See bolded area
The proposed project is a placer mine on 351 claims along Kluane Lake, Gladstone Creek and tributaries (Cranberry Creek, Cyr Creek, Audery Pup, Hannah Pup, and 7 no-name pups), within the White River watershed. The Project is located approximately 10 km from Destruction Bay across Kluane Lake, and within Kluane First Nation (KFN) and White River First Nation Territories. The project overlaps Settlement Land parcel KFN R-18B and is adjacent to KFN Settlement Land parcel KFN R-13A. No work is proposed on Settlement Land parcels. The existing Cultus Bay Road will be used to access the claims from Silver City; this road overlaps Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Territory. Project is also accessible by boat over Kluane Lake between Destruction Bay and Gladstone Creek. Project activities will occur between February 1 and December 15 annually for a period of 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the creation of a Safety Rest Area (SRA) at km 467 of the Robert Campbell Highway, approximately 53 km west of Faro and 99 km east of the Carmacks. Construction of the SRA is to occur within the highway Right-Of-Way and within a portion of an existing gravel reserve. Project activities will occur > 20 m away from the nearest waterbody and are proposed to take place in the summer or fall of 2024. The Project is anticipated to take between 3 and 8 weeks to complete with overall impacts to traffic expected to be minimal. The project is located within the Traditional Territory of the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation, the Ross River Dena Council, and the Liard First Nation.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a class 3 quartz exploration program in the Sixty Mile region. The Project will consist of a total of 335 claims and will be accessed via the Top of The World Highway and Sixty Mile Road and by aircraft. The Project is located within Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory, approximately 65 km west of Dawson City and falls within Land Management Unit 15: Khel Dëk (Sixty Mile) and Unit 21: Wëdzey Tąy (Fortymile Caribou Corridor) of the Recommended Dawson Land Use Plan. Only two claims (Rod 3 and Rod 5) within LMU 21 will have low impact work, no work will occur in other areas of LMU 21. Project activities are proposed to occur year-round for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing a heli-supported quartz mineral exploration program on 54 claims on the Ice Project, located north of the Robert Campbell Highway, approximately 60 km east of Ross River. The Ice Property, located in southeastern Yukon, will be accessed via helicopter from a staging location along the Robert Campbell Highway. The Project is within the Territories of the Ross River Dena Council and the Liard First Nation. Activities including drilling, core storage, down-hole geophysical surveying, sampling, access construction and use, clearing, helicopter use, establishment of a camp, water use, fuel storage, waste management, and reclamation. Activities will occur seasonally from May 1 through September 30, annually for five years.

projectScope.summary: The proposed Project is for the renewal of a Parks Canada License of Occupation (LOO) for a seasonal camp, consisting of two weatherhaven tents, in Kluane National Park and Reserve. The Project is located within Kluane and White River First Nations Traditional Territories. The camp will be located on a glacier near Mt. Queen Mary and will be occupied on a seasonal basis from May 1 to August 15 for five years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on seven claims on Little Skookum Gulch, a tributary to Bonanza Creek, in the Klondike River watershed. The Project is located approximately 15 km south of Dawson City, within the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Traditional Territory, and the Land Management Units 11: Goldfields. Some project activities are proposed to occur on Parks Canada’s Discovery Claim Reserve. The site is accessed by existing roads, including Bonanza Creek Road. Project activities are proposed to occur from April 1 to November 30, annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The purpose of the Project is to upgrade the existing North 60 Petro Ltd. fuel service station and bulk fuel plant at 1257 Alaska Highway in the Town of Watson Lake. The Project includes replacing the existing bulk fuel plant with a new fuel plant containing four – 120 000 litre (L) tanks. The current fuel service station will be upgraded with new pumps and one – 120 000 L storage tank, for a total storage capacity of 600 000 L. The bulk fuel storage will contain diesel and gasoline. The bulk fuel plant will be constructed within a secondary containment consisting of a lined and bermed facility. The Project is located within Kaska Territory (Liard First Nation and Ross River Dena Council as defined by YESAA). Project activities will begin in Spring or Fall 2026, with construction taking approximately four months to complete.

The Project is located approximately 25 km southeast of Dawson, within Land Management Unit 12: Tr’ondëk Täk’it (Klondike Valley) of the Final Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory. projectScope.summary: The Project is the operation of commercial guided river trips, day hiking and associated activities along the Alsek River, within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR). The Alsek River trips start near Haines Junction at Serpentine Creek or at the Dezadeash River bridge and end in Dry Bay. Alsek River trips will use a helicopter for the Turnback Canyon portage. The Proponent will operate annually for five years, running multi-day trips between June 1 and September 15.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of placer mining on 98 claims along Barker Creek, Dixie Gulch, and the first Unnamed Left Limit Tributary of Barker Creek (UNLLT 1), in the Stewart River Placer Watershed. The Project is located approximately 101 km south of Dawson City and will be accessed via barge and air from Dawson City to the Thistle Creek barge landing (Yukon River), Barker Creek barge landing (Stewart River), and the Thistle Creek and Barker Creek airstrips. The Project is located within the Territories of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and Selkirk First Nation, and the Asserted Territory of the White River First Nation. Project activities are scheduled to occur from March 15 to November 15, annually, for 10 years.

The Project is the construction of a multi-use trail in the Dawson City Midnight Dome area. It is located in Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory, approximately 1.5 km from Dawson City. The trail will be a two-way hiking and one-directional climbing bike trail between the existing Acklen Ditch trail and the Midnight Dome summit trail. The trail will be up to 2.4 km long and 1.5 m wide. Final dimensions and routing will be determined during the construction phase. The Project will be accessed via existing roads and trails in the Midnight Dome area and use two existing areas on public access roads for staging project activities. Project activities are anticipated to start on June 1, 2025, and be completed by September 30, 2025.

The Project is located on Lot 100 in the community of Mayo, which is located within First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun Territory. The Project involves the continued operation of three 1 MW diesel generators for the purpose of backup power generation during power outages or shortages. projectScope.activities:
No camp proposed

2-4 workers staying in private accommodations

Use of heavy equipment

vehicles and equipment will be routinely inspected and repaired as required to eliminate leaks
equipment will be inspected and cleaned prior to moving to the project area to prevent the spread of invasive vegetation

Timber harvesting

cutting of up to 14 963 m3 standing green and dead trees 

30% in-stand retention
streams and wetlands buffered by at least 100 m
each tree to be cut between Feb 1 and Apr 30 will be inspected for stick nests indicating the potential presence of nesting gray jays or owls (i.e. early nesting birds); if active stick nests are found, tree-cutting within 50 m will be postponed until nestlings have fledged 
raptor tree nests or stick nests will not be destroyed; a minimum 50 m no activity buffer will be in place when harvesting activities occur between Aug 1 and Apr 30, unless the nest is determined to be inactive by a qualified professional
while tree harvesting or ground clearing, the work area shall be routinely monitored for the nests of ground and shrub nesting birds between May 1 and July 31 and if found and occupied by nesting birds, tree-cutting within 50 m will be postponed until such time as the young have fledged

skidding trees to landing/staging area(s)
processing trees (limbing, cutting to length)
scattering and/or mechanically piling of debris
burning mechanically-piled debris when snow cover is present on the ground
hauling wood from the project site with vehicles ranging from pickups to large multi-axle trucks

Access

seasonal maintenance (deadfall clearing, snow removal, grading or light maintenance) on existing roads and trails
construction of class 4, light haul forest resource roads

up to 5 km total x 12 m clearing width for a total of 6 ha
access to new forest resources roads will be restricted to licensees and permitted personnel during operations
if winter-road snow plowing occurs, a gap in the snowbanks will be created periodically to allow for unhindered wildlife travel

use of skid trails 

up to 10 km x 4 m width for a total of 4 ha
harvesting of trees may/will occur on skid trails

clearing of 6 landing/staging area(s) for a total of 3 ha
no water crossings

low-lying drainage areas may require improvement (e.g., corduroy and/or culverts) if snow-free season access is to occur
access across wetlands will be restricted to periods of frozen ground with a minimum of 20 cm snow depth


Fuel use and storage: up to 500 L

two-200 L slip tanks of diesel on truck beds
up to five-20 L jerry cans of gasoline on truck beds
fuel will be stored within a leak containment structure or upon a non-porous substrate material
spill prevention and mitigation plan will be implemented prior to activities
readily available spill containment equipment will be on-site 
fuel storage and refueling > 30 m from watercourse

Reclamation of trails, roads, landing and harvest areas

reforestation and silviculture activities 
scarification of roads, trails, landings and harvest areas; covering with forest debris to promote regeneration

Other

The Proponent shall review “Handbook for Identification of Heritage Sites and Features” (Yukon Tourism and Culture, 2005) to aid in the recognition of heritage sites and features
If suspected heritage sites or features are discovered during project activities, the operator must immediately suspend operations and the heritage departments of Carcross/Tagish First Nation and Taku River Tlingit First Nation in addition to YG Tourism and Heritage, shall be contacted and activities in the area suspended until provided further direction
Signage shall be installed along the public road indicating commercial vehicle and equipment use in the area during periods of active operations 
The Proponent shall notify the trapping concession holder prior to undertaking activities when project activities either: occur during legal trapping seasons, or will overlap with and impact existing access points, trails, trap sets or other concession-related infrastructure 
The Proponent shall work with the trapping concession holder to identify solutions that reduce or eliminate potential land use conflicts between timber harvesting activities and trapping activities
If wildlife, such a moose or caribou, are encountered during operations, activities will be suspended until such time as the wildlife has left the immediate area; caribou observations will be reported to the Southern Lakes Regional Biologist
The permittee shall routinely monitor the work area for indications of the presence of bat hibernacula or maternal roosts and, if found a no disturbance buffer of 50 m will be maintained around hibernacula. If a maternal roost is found, tree-cutting will be postponed until the young have fledged. If a bat hibernaculum or maternal roost is found the Proponent will contact the Department of Environment, Conservation Data Centre.
Bear dens will not be disturbed or damaged
The Proponent shall maintain a 25-m retention buffer around inactive black bear dens if discovered
The Proponent shall implement a no disturbance buffer zone of 100 m for an individual denning black bear and 200 m for a denning sow with cubs; the no disturbance buffer zone shall remain in place until the bear(s) naturally leave the den in spring, and a 25-m retention buffer shall be maintained

projectScope.summary: The Project is for the decommissioning and demolition of one two-story, wood-framed building. The building consists of five living units and a central living area and is no longer suitable for use. The Project is located within the Stewart Crossing Highway Maintenance Camp, adjacent to the North Klondike Highway in the settlement of Stewart Crossing. The Project will take place over 12 weeks during the snow-free months of either 2024 or 2025. The Project is located within the Traditional Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer operation located on four separate sites, spanning 10 non-contiguous claims along Bonanza Creek, Spring Gulch, Adams Creek, Upper Bonanza Creek and an unnamed right limit tributary (UNRLT) in the Klondike River watershed. The Project is located approximately 3.5 km southeast of the City of Dawson, within Land Management Units 13: Ch’ë̀nyä̀ng (City of Dawson), and 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in (TH) Territory. The Project overlaps TH Settlement Land parcel TH-R20A, however no activities will occur on the parcel. Access to the Project will occur via Bonanza Road, Upper Bonanza Road and Grand Forks Road. Project activities will occur between January 1 to December 31, annually, for up to 10 years.*bold text below denotes a clarification/change in project scope

Timber greater than 150 mm in diameter at breast height will be stockpiled for local salvage
Forestry Branch best practices guidelines will be followed (i.e. merchantable timber is a minimum of 125  mm diameter at breast height at the height of 1.3 m from the base, 80 mm in diameter at the top and contains 50% sound wood)
Where possible, only hand tools (e.g., chainsaw) will be used to remove vegetation within 10 m of the natural boundary of any waterbody or watercourse

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the reconstruction of 46 km of the North Klondike Highway (#2) from km 666 to km 712, between the limits of Dawson City and 132 km north of Stewart Crossing. Activities will include resurfacing, widening, and realignment of the road, as well as drainage and safety improvements and granular development. New granular pit development will be at km 647, km 654.2, and km 657. Granular pits at km 670.3, km 671.7, km 694.3 and km 706.7 are existing pits to be assessed as their development predated YESAB. Existing gravel pits at km 651.5, km 662.8, km 706.1 of the North Klondike Highway, and km 1.1 of the Dempster Highway have been assessed but are included in the scope as they may serve as camp locations. The reconstruction of the highway is planned to occur over the next 25 years, seasonally between April 1 and November 30. The granular reserves development and production are proposed to happen over the next 50 years. The temporal scope of the Project is considered to be 50 years.
* The scope was amended on March 03, 2025. Change to the scope are in bold (See document YOR # 2024-0121-0047).
Project scope changes were made as a result of the March 4, 2025, response from Proponent (YOR 2024-121-0157) and are bolded below.
*The scope was amended on March 06, 2025. Pit # 8 at km 674.5 of the North Klondike Highway has been removed from the scope.
* The scope was amended on April 23, 2025, following the review of the Information Request # 5 (YOR 2024-0121-0086). Changes are in bold. Pit # 10 at km 676, Pit # 11 km 685.3 and Pit # 12 km 687 were removed from the scope and hours of work were added. Additionally, commitments regarding blasting activities at the quarry at Pit # 13 km 694.3 were added.

projectScope.summary: The proposed project is a year-round commercial wilderness tourism operation within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR). Activities include a combination of day and multi-day paddling and hiking. Project activities occur within Champagne and Aishihik First Nations, Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation Traditional Territories. The Proponent will operate annually for five years, starting in 2024. The season of operation will be from May to September for the summer season and from October to May for the winter season.

Up to 2 new fords on UNRLT # 3
Crossings daily with small equipment and 1-2 times a month for heavy equipment 

Drilling (Auger/ RC or Sonic drilling)

20 holes/claim/year (maximum 100 holes/year)
Up to 25 m2 of clearing per drill site 
Holes plugged and backfilled after completion
No use of water or additives

Test Pits

Up to 10 per claim (up to 2 m W x 5 m L and  5 m D)
Backfilled upon completion

Use of Timber

Stockpiled, burned, limbed/bucked and dispersed, used for mining activities and reclamation
Clearing is done outside of the nesting season (early May to Late August) 

Overburden Removal

Up to 450 000 m3/year in total, including:

Hydraulic removal: up to 50 000 m3/year
Mechanical removal: up to 400 000 m3/year

Overburden piles up to 6 m high, stored >20 m away from watercourses, 2:1 slope when possible

Material Processing: up to 200 000 m3/year

Mining cuts sloped 2:1

Water Use

Up to 30 000 m3/day in total from Sulphur Creek, UNLLT #2, UNRLT #2 and groundwater, for the purposes of sluicing (up to 30 000 m3/day), hydraulic stripping (up to 10 000 m3/day) and camp use (up to 2 m3/day)
Up to 90% recycled; makeup water 4000 m3/day
All pump intakes will use fish screens

Reservoirs

Out-of-stream reservoirs (up to 60 x 60 x 5 m, 1 m freeboard) and will include a bypass channel
In-stream dug-outs (up to 5 x 5 x 2 m)

Settling Facilities

Out-of-stream (approximately 60 x 60 x 5 m, 2 m freeboard)

Bermed with overburden and coarse tailings

Settling facilities and mine cuts contoured to allow wildlife egress

Diversions (seasonal, temporary and permanent) 

Up to 7 660 m total (Sulphur Creek: up to 3 800 m, tributaries: up to 3860)

Fuel Storage 

Stored > 80 m from watercourse
Diesel: up to 41 800 L (Double-walled tanks and Tidy Tank)
Gas: up to 5 820 L (drums and double-walled tank)
Propane: up to 2500 lbs (tanks)
Refueling equipment > 30 m from watercourse; water pump refueled >2 m from watercourse
Water pumps placed on a fuel containment tray located above the ordinary high-water mark 
Stationary tanks stored in either double-walled Enviro-Tank or lined and bermed enclosure with 110% capacity
Spill contingency plan provided, spill kits kept on-site
Equipment stored on-site during the off-season

Use of two camps (for up to 12 people per camp, totaling 24 people)

One existing camp (Claim 3 P 08204) and one new camp (Lil 12 P 07998)

Up to 10 trailers, 6 RVs, 10 Sea Cans and use of existing approved septic systems and another approved septic system to be installed
> 30 m away from any watercourse


Waste Management

Waste stored in a secured place and disposed of at the Dawson City landfill on a routine basis
Kitchen waste incinerated daily in barrels (< 5 kg/day); ash buried over 100 m from watercourse
Hazardous waste stored in safe containers, > 30 m from watercourse until transported to an approved facility 

Operational Considerations

Heritage Resources discovered will be marked, protected with a 30 m buffer and reported to First Nations
Follow the guidance in “Guidelines for Industrial Activity in Bear Country” and wildlife deterrent kept on site
District Conservation Officer will be contacted if bears are observed in the project area

Progressive and Final Reclamation
projectScope.summary: The Project is a placer mining operation on 27 claims on Sulphur Creek and tributaries within the Indian River Watershed, approximately 65 km southeast of Dawson City. The Project is located within the First Nation Territories of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun and Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and falls within LMU 11 (Goldfields) of the Dawson Planning Region. Access to the area is by the existing Hunker-Sulpher-Granville loop road from Dawson City. Operations are proposed from April 1 through November 1, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.activities:
Project Scope was updated January 23, 2026 - See bolded area
Access

Helicopter Access 

Up to five round trips per day between camp, fuel cache and north saddle drill area from May 15 to September 14

Approximately 8 hours/day of flying time
Up to 20 drill sites accessed by helicopter

Up to 4 new 50 m2 heli-pad (200 m2 total)

No vegetative mat will be removed

Intermittent helicopter use to facilitate late winter water quality monitoring 
No Helicopter use between September 15 and October 15 

On-claim Roads and Trails

Existing on-claim roads (11 km)
Existing 3 km of on-claim trails 
New temporary on-claim trails (2 km x 4 m wide)
Existing trails used to access areas of interest

Fords

Use of five existing fords

On YF47476, YB41148, YB42176, YB422178 and YB42184 

Crossing frequency

Light vehicles: up to 3 crossings per week for general camp for general camp expediting, including food, materials, and outgoing sample shipments and up to 6 crossings per day while drilling the North Saddle area
Heavy equipment as required



Drilling

Ground-based and heli-supported drilling 
Diamond drilling up to 150 drill holes totaling 60 000 m in length

Up to 25 drill holes per claim, maximum 50 drill holes per year
When practical, low-impact track-mounted drills and support equipment will be used to minimize ground disturbance
All drill holes marked with a location post or flagging and stemmed or plugged to minimize a groundwater infiltration path
No drill holes or sumps within the 30 m ordinary high-water mark of a waterbody/water course
Only biologically inert and biodegradable drilling muds and additives will be used during the drilling process
Drilling fluids will be directed to a natural sump or constructed sump to allow the drill fluid to naturally exfiltrate
Drilling fluids will be re-circulated when possible and all drill fluids will be contained within the sump

Core storage

All drill cores will be secured and stored in accordance with Section 27 of the Yukon Quartz Mining Land Use Regulation, to prevent physical disturbance or collapse
Core boxes covered with plywood or a similar durable material


Trenching

Up to 10 trenches; total volume of 1 800 m3

Up to 30 m x 3 m x 2 m
Cut with mechanized equipment
Trenches sloped at one end to prevent wildlife entrapment
Trenches reclaimed upon completion of geological mapping and sampling 


Vegetation Clearing for Exploration

Drilling (60 drill sites)

121 m2 of vegetation clearing per drill site 

7 260 m2 total 

64 m2 of each drill site will require removal of vegetative mat

 3 840 m2 total

If vegetative mat is present, it will be removed and stored separately, then replaced after seasonal operations
Most proposed/possible drill sites located well above the tree line and do not require any preparation

New corridors for water lines: up to 4000 m2

No disturbance of the vegetative mat
Corridors will only be brushed out and cutting trees greater than 10 cm in diameter will be avoided

Lichen-rich areas avoided 

If lichen is disturbed, it will be retained and replaced as soon as exploration activities stop

Vegetative mat material removed from proposed trails or drill sites will be properly stockpiled, staked, and marked for future reclamation
If the vegetative mat must be removed, it will be stored separately from overburden or bedrock to protect seed and rootstock for re-establishment after exploration
Clearing native vegetation and topsoil will be minimized to reduce impacts on the surrounding ecosystem
Residual vegetation will be left nearby where it is possible to serve as seed sources and propagule banks
Progressive reclamation of clearings when no longer required

Water Use

Up to 203.5 m3/day sourced from nearby watercourses

Water for camp use: up to 3.5 m3/day
Water for drilling: up to 15 gallons per minute or 200 m3 per day 

Sourced from surface run-off and nearby creek headwater run-off and managed using natural or constructed sumps 
Sumps: 2 m long x 1 m wide and 1 m deep
Sumps constructed with sloped sides or ramps to provide wildlife egress or secured with fencing or netting to prevent wildlife access
All sumps marked during use and then backfilled and reclaimed at the end of each season


Appropriate fish screens and/or guards used on intake pumps
All pumps housed in a secondary containment structure
Apply best practices described in Best Management Practices for Works Affecting Water in Yukon to ensure protection of water quality
Water flow downstream of any water intake or extraction points will be monitored to ensure there is sufficient flow to avoid alteration, disruption, or destruction of fish habitat
Monitoring of water flow downstream of any water intake or extraction points 

Existing Camp: Up to 30 people

One existing camp located on claim YC02291; up to 3 000 person days per year
Existing 3 000 m2 camp; no clearing required for camp establishment
All grey water collected in a sump downwind and away from camp
Camp consists of wall tents, kitchen, dining area, first aid area, privies, generator shacks
Potable water sourced from surface water on YB42184

Drinking water will comply with the Canadian Drinking Water Quality Guidelines

Water treated by filtration


Consultation with the Regional Biologist and the Conservation Officer planned to help determine the level of bear activity in the area and the need for an electrified fence
Seasonal camp closure

All camp structures kept locked and/or secured
Signage posted with company contact information at the camp entrance
Wooden tent platforms left in place with all tents and tarps removed from site
All food, garbage and other wildlife attractants removed and disposed of at appropriate facilities in town


Waste Management 

All waste temporarily stored at designated locations secured with bear fencing at the camp or work site prior to off-site disposal

Camp and kitchen waste double bagged and stored in a secure bear proof container at least 30m downwind from camp or incinerated

Up to 30 kg of burnable waste incinerated at minimum every two days
Ash disposed of in an ash pit covered with gravel or local colluvium

Non-incinerable waste transported to appropriate facilities in Mayo or Dawson and disposed of 1-2 times per week
Waste petroleum products (oil, lubricants, and contaminated fuel) temporarily stored in suitable containers at a secured location until the waste can be transported to a designated waste petroleum recycling facility 

All waste petroleum products removed from the site on an annual basis to the nearest recycling facility



Fuel Storage: Up to 16 000 L

Up to 10 000 L of diesel, 4 000 L of Jet B fuel, 2 000 L of gasoline, 1 500 lb of propane stored off-site and on-site
Off-site Storage

Up to 8 000 L of diesel in Envirotank

Envirotank supplied by a Dawson-based fuel company and transported empty to a flat, gravel-filled laydown area on claim YE99505 (not held by the Proponent)
A registration for the Envirotank will be obtained from the Dawson Mining Land Use office prior to positioning the tank
Diesel delivered by bulk fuel supplier to the Envirotank at the laydown area
Envirotank delivered and removed annually from the Big Creek laydown area by the fuel supply contractor

Diesel transferred to drums and trucked or slung by helicopter to a fuel berm 300 m west and below the camp (on-site storage)

On-site Storage

Main fuel storage area (300 m west of the camp): Up to 6 400 L of fuel in sealed drums within lined berm area

Jet B fuel: up to 3 400 L (17 drums)
Diesel: up to 1 400 L (seven drums)
Gasoline: up to 1 600 L (eight drums)

Drill sites: Up to 600 L (three drums) of diesel stored in a lined berm area
In camp/helicopter pad: Up to 3 100 L

Jet B fuel: up to 600 L (three drums)
Diesel: up to 600 L (three drums)
Gasoline: up to 400 L (drums or Tidy Tanks)
Propane: up 1 500 lb 


Fuel delivered to site by certified drivers 
Fuel stored > 50 m away from waterbodies
Helicopter refueled at the camp heli-pad
Spill Contingency Plan in place and appropriate spill kits available at all locations where storage, re-fueling and/or fuel transfer will occur
Fuel use monitored to leave as little fuel as possible on site at season end 

Any remaining fuel Diesel or Jet B left in sealed drums in the lined berm area

All vehicles and equipment will be routinely inspected for leaks and kept in clean and good working condition
Re-fueling will occur near the fuel storage area and servicing of trucks and equipment will not occur near any watercourse
Any contaminated soil from spills will be excavated (shovels) and deposited in a KBL bag for relocation at the end of the season to an approved facility to accept the contaminated soil

Use of Heavy equipment
Reclamation

All disturbances reclaimed when no longer required
Some drill pads will not be reclaimed

Select pads will remain in place to support efficient and environmentally responsible exploration planning over multiple field seasons, for example drilling from the same area


Other

Implementation of the following plans and Polices:

Wildlife Management Plan

Project personnel must adhere to maximum posted speed limits of 35 kmph on all Project roads and travel to road and weather conditions
The operation of all vehicles shall be confined to roads and trails
Clearing of new areas will be avoided during the bird nesting window
If clearing of a new area from the beginning of May to mid-August is unavoidable, one week prior to clearing and creating any new disturbance, notify the Project supervisor. Bush clearing can occur once clearance has been received from qualified environmental personnel
A qualified biologist will assess the presence and/or occupancy of bird nests for proposed disturbances prior to the start of work to enable avoidance of the area. Any other wildlife features observed during the bird nest survey will also be identified and recorded
An avoidance-based approach for migratory bird nests will be implemented, including cessation of disruptive activities, species-based buffer zones with no planned activity until young have left the vicinity of the nest

Ground Disturbance Management Plan

All surface disturbances will comply with the Operating Conditions contained in Schedule 1 of the Quartz Mining Land Use Regulation
Exploration work where possible will be planned to utilize existing access or open areas wherever possible to minimize new disturbances
Access trails requiring construction will be built carefully to avoid grubbing and blading
Use sumps, silt fencing, ditching, re-sloping/re-contouring, and revegetation to control erosion
Arrange windrows of slash perpendicular to slopes to trap sediment
Clean hay bales used with silt fencing to create more resilient sediment traps
Construction of benches on slopes to control erosion
Construction of drainage ditches along the bottom of highly erosive piles or slopes to carry silt-laden run-off to sediment traps (sumps)
Maintain vegetated buffer strips or setbacks between natural stream boundaries and project works
Riparian buffers will be left intact, with no works occurring within 30 m of a water body

Caribou Management Plan

The Project will interact with caribou during the fall rut. However, the strict operating season ending September 15 will ensure limited to no interactions occur
Caribou will always have the right-of-way. Vehicle operators may resume driving once caribou have crossed the road of their own accord
Project personnel are prohibited from encouraging caribou to cross the roads or from scaring them away from the road (e.g., honk the horn) except under rare circumstances where failure to do so could result in harm to caribou or project personnel
If caribou are encountered in an active work area (e.g., drill site, land clearing), all operational activities shall be minimized or ceased according to operational requirements, such as the health and safety of project personnel. Work may resume to full activity once the caribou have left the work area on their own accord
Project personnel cannot approach or encourage caribou to leave the vicinity of an active worksite except under rare circumstances where failure to do so could result in harm to caribou or project personnel. Any known attempts to harass caribou will be subject to corrective actions
When caribou are observed on an active claim (e.g., where high-intensity activities are occurring) during caribou-sensitive frames (fall rut), activities will be suspended until the caribou leave the area on their own
Will comply with Flying in Caribou Country guidelines (e.g. appropriate altitudes for specific timing windows and for when caribou are observed, adjustment of flight routes when caribou are in area, helicopters will not be used to view caribou, shortened helicopter use window to prevent disturbance to caribou in project area
The Project will provide all seasonal flight path operations annually to Yukon Government
Project infrastructure that is not directly related to exploration activities will be located outside preferred caribou habitats wherever possible, as well as outside of a 1 000 m buffer from mineral licks
Potential road and trail routes will be reconnoitered to select paths that minimize disturbance to high-value or sensitive caribou habitats (e.g., high-use trails, dense lichen mats)
The Proponent has provided a decision tree for vehicle response associated with caribou road crossings (caribou within 100 m of road, caribou beyond 100 m of road)

Violence and Harassment Policies

The Florin Gold Project will provide a workplace for all employees, contractors, clients, and visitors that is free from violence by establishing preventative measures, holding perpetrators of the acts accountable and by aiding and support to those who are impacted by workplace violence
Employer will provide treatment provision to prevent / reduce the potential for a worker to suffer an acute reaction (i.e. mental stress) to a traumatic event would be required, in confidence, by the employer. Employer is required to advise any worker who is subject to violence or who witnesses a violent incident of the availability of immediate debriefing
Employees shall place safety as their highest concern and shall report all acts of violence and threats of violence
All reports of violence will be handled in a confidential manner, with information released only on a need-to-know basis
Implementation of hostile actions standard operating procedures that requires employees to report threats immediately, follow supervisor guidance, engage emergency services as needed, complete incident reports, and participate in debriefings and training to ensure personal safety

Heritage Resources

If heritage finds are discovered, work will cease, the area marked with a minimum 30 m buffer, and the Project Supervisor will be contacted
If any heritage resources are discovered the Heritage Department of the Na-Cho Nyak Dun First Nation and the Yukon Government’s Heritage Branch will be notified
Adherence to applicable legislation to minimize effects to heritage resources (Historic Resources Act (s. 64), Archeological Sites Regulation (s.4), FNNND FA CH 13 – Heritage, Quartz Mining Act, Quartz Mining Land Use Regulations (Section E)
Adherence to the following documents the Mineral Exploration Best Management Practices for Heritage Resources, Best Management Practices for Burial Sites and Human Remains in Yukon
Care will be taken to avoid disturbance of artifacts and sites of heritage and historical significance
Future areas that have not been examined by the HROA and HRIA will proactively manage and mitigate any disturbance to historical and/or heritage sites

Wetlands

Conducting work within ecologically sensitive areas such as wetlands will be avoided

Other Users

Signage will be posted on the road at the project boundary to inform potential hunters that there is a camp and active exploration in the area
At least thirty (30) days prior to each field season and communicate the timing of exploration activities to the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun and other land and resource users that may access the project area. Florin will work with these individuals and governments to identify and resolve potential land use conflicts and any potential effects on other users
Site personnel will also adhere to a no hunting policy that will eliminate any competition with local hunters
Care will be taken to not disturb or scatter wildlife in the area during exploration activities and air support will avoid low passes or hovering over game animals and hunting parties

Biodiversity

To prevent the spread of invasive species and potential soils, all equipment entering the site must be clean of soils and seeds. The equipment will be inspected prior to entering the project area and then allowed to enter
projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a ground-based and heli-supported quartz exploration program on 500 claims known as the Florin Gold Project, in central Yukon. It is located approximately 55 km northwest of Mayo, within the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. The Project is accessed via an existing 95 km network of existing roads (Clear Creek – Big Creek – Hobo Creek roads) connecting to the Klondike Highway at km 610. Project activities will take place from May 15 to September 15, annually, for a period of 5 years. Late winter water quality monitoring will also occur intermittently during this time.

projectScope.summary:
The project scope was updated on November 19, 2025 to show both the combined and separate project activities for each site. 
The Project is a grouped project of three individual placer mines on 374 claims along tributaries of Henderson Creek in the Yukon River North Watershed. The Project is located approximately 140 km south of Dawson City in the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory. The Project falls within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields – Integrated Stewardship Area 4, of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The Project will be accessed via the Goldfields road network that extend southwest from the Hunker-Granville-Sulphur Loop (Route #312), and over the Eureka Dome and Henderson Dome, using existing roads. The Project will involve the creation of new roads and trails. Project activities are proposed to occur from April to November, annually, for 12 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 8 claims, along Sulphur Creek and Right Limit No-Name Pup in the Indian River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 52 km southeast of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. Project activities will occur between April 15 to October 15, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a placer mine on 212 claims along Scroggie Creek, Mariposa Creek and Stevens Creek and an UNRLT in the Stewart River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 105 km west northwest of Pelly Crossing in the Traditional Territories of the Selkirk First Nation and Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in. The Project will be accessed via fixed wing airplane or along a winter access road used intermittently over the 10-year period. Years that require use of winter road access will start as early as February 20. All other activities will take place from April 15 till October 15, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the continuation of a placer mining operation on 239 claims along Montana Creek and tributaries in the Indian River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 45 km southeast of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Territories of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. The Project will be accessed via an existing network of roads, including the Whitehorse Overland Trail. Project activities will occur between April 1 and October 31, annually, for up to 10 years.
*Scope changes are in bold and are from IR 2 Response (YOR# 2025-0087-0042). The Map Package has been updated accordingly.

projectScope.summary: The purpose of the project is to construct and operate a new community water treatment plant (WTP) facility on land adjacent to the Lorne Mountain Community Centre with access directly off Annie Lake Road. Construction of the new WTP is planned to commence in 2025 and is expected to be commissioned in 2026. The facility is designed for an operating life of approximately 25 years. The project location is in the community of Mt. Lorne approximately 31 km north of Carcross, within the traditional territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Carcross/Tagish First Nation.

projectScope.summary: The Project is an amendment to an existing placer mine (water licence PM23-007) previously assessed by the Designated Office in 2022 (YESAB project 2022-0163). The 2022 assessment included 62 placer claims and a section of a lease, named the “1 mile” lease in the 2022 Project that measures 0.5 miles. This Project is an assessment of an additional 1.5 miles to the previously assessed 0.5 mile section of lease, for a total 2 mile lease (approximately 21 claims along baseline) on Serpentine Creek. The 0.5 mile section of lease lapsed before the water licence PM23-007 was authorized and was removed from that licence. This assessment includes all project activities on the full 2 mile lease. The Project is located in the White River Watershed and is approximately 35 km northwest of the community of Burwash Landing, and within the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation Territories. Project activities will occur between March 15 and September 15 annually, until the licence expires June 10, 2034.

projectScope.summary:
Castle Rock Enterprises is proposing the renewal of a quarry lease (Disposition #: 960239) and the expansion of operations at the existing Castle Rock quarry to ensure a stable supply of gravel for infrastructure projects in Whitehorse.  The Project is located along the Haeckel Ski Area Road, approximately 13 km northwest of downtown Whitehorse, within the municipal boundaries. The Project includes ongoing operations, utilizing a mobile concrete recycling crusher and plant, a quarry expansion of 50 m in the north direction (approximately a 2.79 ha increase), and the installation and operation of an asphalt plant. The lease area to be renewed has a total area of 68.54 ha. Project activities will occur from May to November annually, with operating hours from 7 am to 7 pm. The Project is anticipated to operate for 10 years, with site reclamation to be completed in 2035. The proponent expects an additional 300 000 m3 of aggregate available before the resource is exhausted.  
The Project is within Kwanlin Dün First Nation territory and is 100 m west of KDFN Settlement Land Parcel C-6B.

Construction of the SRA is to occur within the existing highway Right-Of-Way and is proposed to take place in the summer or fall of 2024. The Project will take place > 20 m away from the nearest waterbody and will take between 3 and 8 weeks to complete. Overall impacts to traffic are expected to be minimal. The project is located within the Traditional Territory of the Teslin Tlingit Council.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continued operation and maintenance of the existing Terus Construction Ltd. Quarry and asphalt plant. The Project was previously assessed in 2014, YOR: 2014-0176, with the current air emission permit expiring on December 31, 2024. The project site is located within the municipal boundaries of Whitehorse. Access to the Project is from Ear Lake Road off Robert Service Way. The Project is located within the Territories of Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta’an Kwäch’än Council. The proposed Project involves the extraction, crushing, hauling of aggregate materials, asphalt production and associated activities. Mining of aggregate reserve over the next 10 years will be at a rate of 10 000 m3 per year. The asphalt plant is expected to produce between 20 000 to 40 000 tonnes of material per year. On-site crushing will take place up to 2 times per year for a period of 1-3 weeks. Proposed activities will occur seasonally between May to the beginning of October and will continue for 10 years (2025-2035)

projectScope.summary:
The Project is an amendment to YESAB Project 2022-0090 for roadworks on the Dempster Highway, aimed at repairing the road prism and adjusting and armoring riverbank areas of the East Blackstone River, Blackstone River, Ogilvie River, and Engineer Creek. The amendment includes the addition of 20 priority sites along the highway that were identified as at risk of destabilization since the Project was initially assessed in 2022. A new pro-active stabilization technique may also be applied at these 20 new priority sites. The 20 sites will be located between kilometres 175 and 243.5, within the 60-metre-wide right-of-way of the Dempster Highway. 
The Project is located approximately 120 km NE of Dawson City by road, within the Territories of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in, Vuntut Gwitchin First Nation, and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. No project areas overlap Settlement Land, but two priority sites are contiguous with Settlement Land parcels (VGFN R-8A and TH S-147B1), and others are located within 500 m of various Settlement Land parcels (VGFN S-56A/D, TH R-49A, TH S-152B1, TH S-182B1, TH S-148B1, TH S-185B, VGFN S-44A1, TH S-180B1, TH S-181B1). The Project is located within the Dempster Highway Corridor sub-regional planning unit of both the Peel Watershed Land Use Plan and Dawson Regional Planning Commission Recommended Plan. Three of 20 sites are also within the Tombstone Corridor of Tombstone Territorial Park. Project activities are anticipated to begin in fall 2024 and continue for 25 years, until October 15, 2049.
Please see YOR Document 2024-0106-0016. This document contains an organizational chart and maps that overview the project activities in relation to Settlement Land parcels.
The Project Scope - Summary was updated on April 10, 2025 to correct an error in the project number of the previously assessed project. The change is in bold.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the relocation of a hydrometric cableway station located approximately 9 km upstream on the West Aishihik River. A hydrometric cableway station is a site where water data is collected (i.e. water level, streamflow, etc.). The current station is no longer in a suitable location and will be decommissioned; a replacement station will be constructed approximately 600 m upstream. Project activities will occur within 5 years after receiving permitting, for up to 10 days, between April and October.
The Project is located within the Traditional Territory of Champagne and Aishihik First Nations, approximately 50 km north-northeast of Haines Junction.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a class 3 quartz exploration program near Clear Creek in the Little South Klondike River drainage. The Project consists of a total of 1 052 contiguous claims over a distance of 44 km that will be accessed via the Clear Creek Road (for approximately 23 km) and by aircraft. The Project is approximately 66 km northwest of Mayo and 119 km southeast of Dawson, within Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun Territory. Part of the project area falls within Land Management Unit 9: Clear Creek of the Recommended Dawson Land Use Plan. Project activities are proposed to occur from June 1 to September 15 annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the development of a residential subdivision to the east and north of 7th Avenue in the Village of Mayo. The subdivision includes the construction of approximately 24 lots identified as single detached, semi-detached, multi-family and rowhouse lots. The total development area (including internal greenspaces and parks) is 2.3 ha. The Project is located within the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun.
Development of the Master Plan area is anticipated to begin as soon as funding is allocated, and permits are obtained. Detailed engineering design, site preparation and construction will occur outside of bird nesting season (early May to October). Clearing of the site is dependent on market forces and available financial resources.

projectScope.summary: The Project is an amendment to an existing Class 3 placer mine on one claim (permit LP01612 issued in 2024), previously assessed by the Dawson Designated Office in 2024 (YOR 2024-0025). The proposed amendment is to increase testing and evaluation via stripping, trenching, tests pits and construction of new roads and trails on Bonanza Creek, in the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 5 km southeast of Dawson City in the Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in. The Project falls within the Land Management Unit 12: Tr’ondëk Täk’it (Klondike Valley), within the Recommended Dawson Land Use Plan. The Project will be accessed via Bonanza Road. Amended activities will take place from March 1 to November 1, annually, until license expiry on July 16, 2034.

projectScope.summary:
*Changes are in bold*
The Project is an amendment to the current water licence for the Ross River ferry crossing, previously assessed by the Designated Office in 2019 (YESAB Project 2019-0085). Proposed amendment activities are the construction of two year-round ramps on the north and south shores of the Pelly River at the existing ferry crossing location. The intent of this Project is to reduce the in-stream work time and effort spent constructing and removing temporary ramps.
The Project is located at the ferry crossing on the Pelly River, in the community of Ross River, in the Territories of the Ross River Dena Council and the Liard First Nation. Construction activities of year-round portions of the ramps are to occur between June and October, when required assessments and permits are in place.
Note:
Seasonal construction and removal of up to 46 m of the north and 12 m of the south access ramps may continue as currently permitted. However, the installation of a 25 m long year-round ramp on the north bank and 10 m long year-round ramp on the south bank will reduce the amount of annual instream work time and effort required. The maximum dimensions of the north and south access ramp, including year-round sections, will remain unchanged from those currently permitted.

projectScope.activities:
Timing and Phasing of Activities

Structure removal activities will be scheduled outside of the campground visitor season
Work will be schedules to occur on hard frozen ground whenever possible
Phase one: will occur during late winter/spring 2026. Activities include:

Site preparation and structure analysis

Expected to require approximately three days
Removing internal/external items (not structures)

Testing for hazardous materials

Phase two: will occur during late winter/spring 2026, exact dates dependent on weather conditions when ground is frozen. Activities include:

Structure removal and disposal based on hazardous testing results

Expected to require up to seven days
Non-hazardous materials: deconstructed and removed via mechanical means across frozen snow
Hazardous materials: professional abatement will be used
Outhouses at the site will be capped and decommissioned


Phase three: will occur during spring/summer 2026. Activities include:

Final cleanup and site remediation (if required)
Ecological monitoring for invasive species

Conducted in snow-free conditions (monitoring duration of summer season)
100 m buffer surrounding the project footprints to capture potential indirect impacts to the adjacent aquatic environments



Structure Removal and Clearing

One structure (200 ft2) and outhouse

Group Two: 

Two structures (360 ft2 and 200 ft2)
Tree clearing: up to 10 m3

One main structure (675 ft2) and one shed (80 ft2)

Due to the proximity to water, additional surveillance monitoring will occur during removal and will only occur during dry or frozen conditions
Erosion control measures shall be implemented prior to start of work in areas 30 m of watercourse
Wastes from demolition, including, rock, silt, cement, grout, asphalt, petroleum product, lumber, vegetation, domestic waste, pesticide, herbicide or any deleterious substance will be placed or allowed to disperse into waterbody
If waste does fall into a waterbody, it will be immediately retrieved, provided worker safety is not compromised, and if removal can be done without excessive disturbance of bottom sediment

Group Four: 

One main structure (288ft2) and one shed (187 ft2)
Tree clearing: up to 10 m3


Debris Removal and Material Transportation

Sorting and piling materials for salvage or disposal
No fill is expected to be used
Ground disturbance will be minimal
Some tree clearing may be required to allow for heavy equipment mobility


Use of Heavy Equipment

Equipment from outside the national park will be washed and clean before arrival and free of contaminants
Equipment will be free of leaks and in good working order
Equipment staging will occur on previously disturbed, compacted areas (i.e. parking lot)
Stored a minimum of 30 m from the high-water mark

Fuel and Waste Management

Fuel Type and quantity

Diesel: up to 400 L
If necessary, fuel storage will be a tidy tank mounted on a truck staged in day-use parking lot a minimum of 30 m away from high-water mark

Refueling to occur a minimum of 30 m away from watercourse, if required

If refueling on-site, location will be flat and compacted ground (pre-existing road/parking lot). Secondary containment (e.g., a duck pond) will be required for any ground-based refueling activities
Spill plan in place and spill kit on-site capable of handling 110 percent of the total fuel
Spill response procedures implemented in the event of a spill and notify designated Parks Canada staff

Waste to be contained and transported to an approved facility (Haines Junction or Whitehorse)
Proper waste management practices will be followed

Secure all food, waste, and debris in wildlife-proof containers, a secure building, or vehicles


Access and Access Control

Equipment laydown and staging on site will be the designated day-use parking lot
Light brushing may be required for heavy equipment access
No ground disturbance or groundwork will be associated with heavy equipment access points
Public access around the project site will be restricted during operations

Protection of Wildlife

Bats

No removal of potential roosting structures will occur outside their active period (April to October)
Visual and audio sweep of the buildings will be conducted by designated park personnel prior to work commencing
Conduct activities during daylight hours and avoid critical foraging times (i.e., dusk and dawn) unless otherwise approved by designated Parks Canada staff 
If dead or injured bats are found, a Parks Canada Environmental Surveillance Officer (or designate) will be notified

Avian Wildlife

Removal of trees and potential nesting structures will occur outside the general nesting period for birds (May 2 to August 20)

If wildlife is observed at or near the work site, the animal(s) will be allowed to leave the work area
Any problems including aggressive encounters with wildlife will be reported immediately to the park Wildlife Conflicts Specialist or designated Parks Canada staff

Heritage Resources

Parks Canada’s Cultural Resource Management (CRM) guidelines will be followed
If a heritage resource is discovered, all work will cease in the immediate area, and appropriate specialists and CAFN will be consulted to assess and document the finding

The Project is located along the Haines Highway, approximately 30 km south of the Village of Haines Junction (Dakwakada), within the Territory of Champagne and Aishihik First Nations (CAFN). projectScope.summary:
The Project is an amendment to the remediation of the BAR-B Beach Refuelling Area, previously assessed by the Dawson Designated Office in 2023 (YOR 2023-0014). The proposed amendment is to increase the volumes of excavated contaminated material and imported granular material to the BAR-B site, to add quarry activities at the BAR-2 Borrow Location, to extend the temporal scope of the Project, and increase the total number of people present at the site.
The Project has two locations: the first, BAR-B Beach Refuelling Area, is the site that requires remediation; the second, BAR-2, is located approximately 80 km southeast of BAR-B and is where quarry activities are proposed to provide infill material to the BAR-B site. The project locations are within the Yukon North Slope and the Inuvialuit Settlement Region. The BAR-B site is also within Ivvavik National Park and is approximately 190 km northwest of Aklavik and 230 km northwest of Inuvik. The BAR-2 site is approximately 120 km northwest of Aklavik and 160 km northwest of Inuvik. The Project’s temporal scope is proposed to extend to September 2026, with activities taking place from mid-June to September in 2025 and late-July to September in 2026.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the continued operation of the Mayo Generating Station (MGS- a hydroelectric generation plant) on the Tadze Nyäk/Mayo River, the construction of a new Mayo Lake Control Structure (MLCS) and associated fish ladder, and the removal of coffer dam materials. There are no proposed changes to the current operating conditions of the Mayo Generating Station, which currently operates under Water Use Licence HY99-012.
The Project regulates flows of water on the Tadze Nyäk/Mayo River and water elevations in Ts'agro Män/Mayo Lake and Wareham Lake in order to produce electricity. The MGS has a generating capacity of 15.7 MW, with 6.5 MW of dependable winter flow, and supplies electricity to the Yukon. The infrastructure associated with the Project includes the Mayo Lake Control Structure (MLCS), the Wareham Lake dam and spillway, Mayo A hydro plant, and Mayo B hydro plant.
The closest Project infrastructure is located approximately 6.5 km north of the Village of Mayo at the outflow of Wareham Lake and overlaps with the Territory of the First Nation of Nacho Nyäk Dun. The temporal scope of this assessment for the operation of the MGS is five years. Construction activities are planned to commence between 2027 and 2029 and may take up to four years to complete.

projectScope.summary:
The Project includes upgrades to water, sanitary and storm sewer infrastructure in the Hillcrest neighbourhood of Whitehorse. Roads will be reconstructed after the new infrastructure has been installed. Phase 1A includes replacing infrastructure on Roundel Road and Summit Road (north of Sunset Drive North), as well as installing a sanitary sewer main and stormwater outfalls in the greenbelt area northeast of Summit Road. Phase 1B includes replacing infrastructure on Sunset Drive North and Summit Road (south of Sunset Drive North), as well as servicing and road frontage upgrades on Lot 130. The project footprint is illustrated in Figure 3. 
The Project occurs within the Traditional Territories of Ta’an Kwäch’än Council and Kwanlin Dün First Nation. Phase 1A project activities will commence during summer 2024 for a period of approximately two weeks. The timing of Phase 1B project activities is subject to funding and is anticipated to take approximately five months in duration and be completed in 2027.

projectScope.activities:
Trail construction (~ 300 m long x 2 m wide)
Clearing and grubbing (~ 0.1 ha)

Clearing for trail construction, display areas, and interpretive area
Trees with >12.5 cm diameter, will be made available for firewood
Woody debris will be chipped and used for landscaping at the site or mulched and dispersed on-site

Clearing is planned for late fall or early spring; however, if it is unavoidable that the site must be cleared during the migratory bird nesting season (early May – late August), then a qualified biologist will survey the work area and identify any nest sites that would be affected by the clearing work

The survey will be conducted no more than 5 days before clearing activities commence, and a buffer zone will be flagged around any encountered nest sites
The size of the buffer zone will be based on the affected bird species, but generally will have a radius of 10 – 25 m (i.e., larger birds such as raptors generally require a larger buffer); no clearing will be allowed within the buffer zone until young birds have fledged

Hauling and staging granular materials for trail surface (~100 m3 of gravel)

Sourced from a commercial gravel pit in Teslin

Relocating historic vehicles to display areas 

8-10 display areas for the placement of vehicles and artefacts along the trail (up to 5 x 5 m each)

Equipment will be kept in good repair and monitored by the contractor and the Historic Sites Project Inspector for fluid leaks
Any equipment that breaks down will be moved off-site for repairs or servicing 
If equipment breaks down, any drips or spills that fall on unprotected ground will be contained and immediately cleaned up with shovels, buckets, and sorbent pads
If equipment requires onsite repair before it can be moved off site, the soil beneath the equipment will be protected using tarps and spill trays; recovered (contaminated) soil will be taken to the YG pit at km 3 South Canol Road, placed on a poly tarp, and covered with another poly tarp until it is tested and can be hauled to a treatment facility
At the end of each day, equipment will be parked at the materials/equipment staging area in the pull-off next to the South Canol Road

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the installation of an interpretive trail and display of WWII-era vehicles to commemorate the Canadian Oil (CANOL) project and will be constructed on a 1.6 ha historic reserve. The site, commonly referred to as the South Canol Truck Dump, is located approximately 50 km east of Teslin, at km 0 on the South Canol Road near the intersection with the Alaska Highway. A new trail, which will be covered with crushed gravel, and eight to ten display areas along the trail will be constructed to display vehicles and artefacts. A central interpretive area with informational signage will be installed at the beginning of the trail. The parking area will be located at the highway rest stop immediately across the road from the reserve.
The Project will be completed in two phases. Phase 1, planned for the spring of 2025, includes clearing for the trail and display areas. Phase 2, planned for the summer of 2025, includes trail construction and placement of historic vehicles. The interpretive signage will be developed in cooperation with the Teslin Tlingit Council and is planned to be in place by 2026.
The Project is located within the Territory of the Teslin Tlingit Council.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine with 77 claims on Unnamed Left Limit Tributary (UNLLT) to Granite Creek in the Mayo River watershed. The project is located approximately 14 km southeast of Keno City and within the Territory of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun First Nation. The Project will be accessed by an existing access road from Keno to Granite Creek area that travels over Sourdough hill, passing near Upper Duncan Creek and over Mount Hinton following the ridge. The Project will take place from April 15th to October 30th annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a quartz exploration program in the Freegold Mountain area. The property consists of 198 claims and will be accessed via the Freegold Road from Carmacks. The Project is located within the Territories of the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation (LSCFN) and Selkirk First Nation (SFN), approximately 57 km northwest of Carmacks. The Project is adjacent to LSCFN Settlement Land parcel LSC R-8A and overlaps with LSC R-9A. No activities will be taking place on Settlement Land. Project activities are proposed to occur from April 1 to November 30, annually, for five years.

projectScope.activities:
Access:

Use of the existing Mush Lake Road
Use of staging area on site (approximately 2000 m2)

2 Helicopter flights (flying for 4 hours each for 2 days) for ignition and fire suppression (equipped with long line and bucket)
Site Preparation:

Site condition monitoring (weather, precipitation, wind, etc.)

Portable weather station set up on-site to monitor weather prior, during and after the prescribed fire

Hose lay set up along Mush Lake Road and sprinkler lines will be set up on the south end and on the east side of Mush Lake Road
Perform a test Fire (north end of the unit) to determine fire behavior and assess the Plastic Sphere Dispenser (PSD) and the Heli-torch prior to ignition

Heli-torch testing done 100 m from riparian zones, water bodies and sensitive areas and use of existing disturbed sites where possible


Ignition

Use of Heli-torch and Plastic Sphere Dispenser (PSD)

PSD: estimated 2000 spheres containing potassium permanganate, stored in Haines Junction

Use of drip torches

Supervision of Burning

Hourly weather reading, and fire behavior monitoring (photos and filming)
Crew will work with hand tools, pumps, hose lay and helicopter with long line and bucket to manage fire lines as a fire holding strategy

Escape Fire Management

Containment zone: fire may be allowed to burn in containment zones; fire outside of containment zone will be declared escape fire
Bucketing followed up by hand crews will be the initial and primary response to all fire outside the zone
Hotspots within 100 feet (30.5 m) of Mush Lake Road will be extinguished by crews

Smoke Management

Residents will be notified prior to operation and ignition
Ignition limited to days with adequate venting conditions
Maximize the flaming phase by mass ignition

Post Burn Procedure

Mop-up operation for up to 4 days (use of hand tools, extinguish hot spots)

Perimeter patrolled and mopped up within 10 m of the active edge
Helicopter use on the 4th day of the mop-up/extinguishment for less than 2hrs (visual monitoring and infrared detection of heat) 
No mop-up will occur on the remaining hot spots if fire hazards is down trending

Use of sprinkler as needed
Use of remote camera and monitor until the end of the season (October)
Fire retardant chemicals not used (unless in an emergency response)

Water Use:

Up to 711 m3 (pumps have a max flow rate of 371 L/min)
All intake hoses of pumps will have fish screens and pumps will maintain a 30 m buffer from spawning habitat

Sprinkler monitored to prevent erosion and soil saturation

Fuel Use and Management

Flash 21 Gelling Agent (up to 20 L), a mix of diesel and gasoline for drip torch (up to 2005 L, 3:2 ratio), a mix of gasoline and oil for fire pumps (up to 200 L, 50:1 ratio) and ethylene glycol (up to 20 L)
Fuel storage, mixing and refueling will not occur within 100 m of streams, wetlands and sensitive sites
Mixing of fuel will take place over an impermeable surface (containment berm) and for refueling of pumps, a mini berm/secondary containment will be built 
Spill kits available on-site and all spills within the containment berm will be immediately cleaned up 

Public Safety

Signs posted on the Haines Road and staff may be present to reduce speed 
Area closure signage

Archeological and cultural resources will be avoided or protected with a buffer zone
projectScope.summary: The Project is a 37-hectare prescribed fire in the southeastern corner of Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR). The Project is located between Mush and Dezadeash lakes, west of the Haines Road, and falls within the Traditional Territory of Champagne and Aishihik First Nations. The ignition of the fire will occur over 1 or 2 days between August 20 and October 31 when conditions allow; one year over the next 5 years. All fuel (trees) adjacent to Mush Lake Road will be sprayed down and a sprinkler line will be running during any ignition. Mop-up/extinguishment and monitoring will occur over several days after the burn.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a quartz mining, processing and production program on 1 claim on Mount Follé. Accessed from Annie Lake Road, the Project lies within in the Hamlet of Mount Lorne, approximately 23 km from Carcross and 45 km from Whitehorse. The Project is located in the Territories of Carcross/Tagish First Nation (C/TFN) and Kwanlin Dün First Nation and overlaps Settlement Land parcel CTFN R-4A. Activities are scheduled to occur annually from May 1 to November 30, for up to 10 years.

The Project is the operation of commercial guided river trips and associated activities along the Alsek River, within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR). Trips start near Haines Junction at Serpentine Creek or the Dezadeash River bridge and end at Dry Bay. Project activities occur within the Traditional Territory of Champagne and Aishihik First Nations. The Proponent will operate annually for five years, running multi-day trips between June and September.
