projectScope.summary: The Project is the establishment and operation of a placer mine on 146 claims, along Wounded Moose[RM1.1] Creek and its tributaries in the Indian River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 60 km South of Dawson City within Land Management Unit 17: Nän Dhòhdäl (Upper Indian River Wetlands) of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Territories of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. Access to the Project will occur by Indian River Road. Project activities will occur between March 1 and November 30, annually, for up to 10 years. Reclamation work will be carried out progressively over the ten-year life of the Project.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the construction and operation of an abattoir and driveway along the Takhini River Road (Lot 1335 Quad 105D/14). The Project is located 45km northwest of Whitehorse, within the Territories of Champagne and Aishihik First Nations, Ta’an Kwäch’än Council, and Kwanlin Dün First Nation. The abattoir will operate between 7:30 AM and 5:00 PM up to two days a week (55 days/year), year-round. Construction is expected to begin as soon as permits are obtained.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a continuation of a placer mine on 59 claims and 1 lease along Quartz Creek and Calder Creek in the Indian River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 35 km south of Dawson City in the Territories of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. The Project falls within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields – Integrated Stewardship Area 4, of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The project location will be accessed via the existing Quartz Creek Road. Project activities are proposed from April 1 to November 1, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The proposed project involves relocating a section of overhead powerline into a highway right-of-way to improve service reliability. projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a quartz exploration program on 92 claims on Barney Ridge, near Clear Creek, approximately 107 km southeast of Dawson City and 70 km northwest of Mayo. The Project is located within the Territories of Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun and falls within Land Management Unit 9: Clear Creek - Integrated Stewardship Area 4 of the Dawson Recommended Land Use Plan. Project activities are proposed from January 1 to December 31, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 131 claims, along Rosebute Creek and tributaries in the Yukon River North Watershed. The Project is located approximately 60 km south of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The Project is located in Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory. Access to the project location will occur via helicopter from Dawson City and by vehicle via the existing Goldfields road network, including Eureka Creek Road and Rosebute Valley Access Road. Project activities will occur between April 1 to October 15, annually, for 10 years.
The Designated Office notes that the Proponent has proposed both Class 3 and Class 4 activities. The Designated Office will assess all activities proposed within one assessment.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the establishment or re-establishment of the Scroggie Creek winter road to transport heavy equipment and materials to a remote placer mine area located on Scroggie Creek. The winter road will be accessed near Pelly Farm via the Pelly Ranch Road, located approximately 37.5 km from Pelly Crossing off the Klondike Highway. The Project is located within the Territories of Selkirk First Nation (SFN), Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in (TH), and First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun (FNNND). A portion of the Project is adjacent to Settlement Land parcel SFN R-20A, and near Settlement Land parcels TH S-209B1, SFN S-86B1/D, SFN S-48B1/D, and SFN R-1B, but no work will occur on Settlement Land. Project activities will occur over up to six trips between December 1 and April 15 (conditions allowing), annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the construction of an access road that will connect to the existing Gully Road to agricultural lot 1527. The Project is located approximately 25 km northwest of downtown Whitehorse and is within the Territories of the Kwanlin Dün, Ta'an Kwäch'än Council and Champagne and Aishihik First Nations. The Project is near or adjacent to settlement land parcels TKC R-9A and KDFN S-365B1 and falls within the Hot Spring Road Local Area Planning boundary. Project activities are expected to take one month, with clearing beginning in March 2026, and the construction of the access road occurring once ground conditions are dry.

The Project is the development of a commercial/industrial subdivision on Lot 429, Lot 426, and Lot 421-138, located approximately 2 km northwest of downtown Whitehorse near the Alaska Highway/Burns Road intersection. The project area is 0.1 km2 (10.1 hectares) in size. The development follows the Wasson Place Extension Master Plan and is located at the south end of the Valleyview South Master Plan area. 
The Project occurs within the Traditional Territories of Ta’an Kwäch’än Council and Kwanlin Dün First Nation. Project activities will occur in two phases over a seven-year period: site preparation for up to five years, followed by subdivision construction for up to two years. Operating hours will be between 7 am – 7 pm daily between May and October each year, as per the City of Whitehorse Maintenance Bylaw and Temporary Land Use permit.

projectScope.summary: The Project is an amendment to an existing Class 4 placer mine on 56 claims (permit PM24-008) along Moose Creek and an Unnamed Right Limit Tributary (UNRLT), in the Forty Mile River Watershed. The Project was previously assessed by the Dawson Designated Office in 2023 (YOR 2023-0134). The proposed amendment is to add in-stream settling, and use of creek as conduit to the existing licence. The Project is located approximately 70 linear km west of Dawson City in Trʼondëk Hwëchʼin Territory. The Project falls within the Wëdzey Tąy (Fortymile Caribou Corridor) Land Management Unit (LMU 21) of the Dawson Planning Region and will be accessed via an existing access road off the Top of the World Highway. Amended activities will take place from April 1 to October 30, annually, until licence expiry on  April 25, 2034.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a continuation of a placer mine on 22 claims along Last Chance Creek, In the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 16 km away from Dawson City in the Traditional Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in. The Project falls within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Dawson Planning Region (LMU 11) and will be accessed via Last Chance Road, an offshoot of Hunker Creek Road. Project activities will take place from March 1 to November 30, annually, for 10 years.

The Project is the continuation of a placer mining operation along 37 claims on Pure Gold Creek, a tributary of Bonanza Creek within the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located within Land Management Unit 11 (Goldfields) of the Recommended Dawson Land Use Plan, within the Traditional Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in, approximately 7 km south-east of Dawson City. The project area is accessed via the Bonanza Creek Road.
The Proponent has operated on this claim block under licence PM16-065 which is set to expire April 4, 2027, and proposes to continue operations from April 1 to November 1 annually, for an additional ten years.

projectScope.summary:
The Proponent is applying for the renewal of Land Use Permit 2019-F889 to move equipment to and from the Proponent's placer mine near Reed Creek during the winter. The winter trail will be constructed along an established ATV trail beginning at Km 1743 on Alaska Highway, approximately 40 km northwest of Burwash Landing. The 13 km trail bypasseses a few small lakes/ponds, then follows a tributary of Reed Creek. The Project is proposed to occur in February /early March (during frozen conditions) over the next 2 years and used during this time, for up to 2 days, annually.
The Project falls within the Territories of Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing a ground and heli-supported quartz mineral exploration program on the Pelly Property, which consists of 422 claims located approximately 130 km southeast of Ross River and 160 km northwest of Watson Lake. projectScope.summary: The proposed Project is a placer mine on 31 claims along Allgold Creek in the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 40 km southeast from Dawson City in the Traditional Territories of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and the First Nation of Na-cho Nyäk Dun. The Project falls within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields and Land Management Unit 10: Tintina Trench, of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The Project will be accessed via the Klondike Highway, by using existing roads, and by creating new roads. Activities are proposed from April 1st to October 31st annually for 10 years. No activities are proposed on Settlement Land or on lands overlapping with other land dispositions.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a placer mine on 248 claims along Thistle Creek and its tributaries within the Yukon River South watershed. The Project is located 110 km south of Dawson City, within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Traditional Territory. The project is located within the Goldfields land management unit under the Dawson Recommended Land Use Plan. The project location will be accessed by river boat/barge and by air at two existing airstrips within and nearby the project area. Project activities are scheduled to occur from May 1 to October 30, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the repair of the existing erosion control structures for the Klondike River Bridge located at kilometer 712.2 of the North Klondike Highway. Project activities will enhance current shoreline stabilization measures to protect the streambank at the Klondike River Bridge from further erosion. The project area is located within Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory, falls within the City of Dawson municipal boundary, and Land Management Unit 13 Ch’ë̀nyä̀ng (City of Dawson) of the Dawson Planning Region. The camp area is located at km 1.1 of the Dempster Highway, within Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory and falls within Land Management Unit 12 Tr’ondëk Täk’it (Klondike Valley) of the Dawson Planning Region.
Work may start any time within five years of the project's authorization and will occur between May 1 and November 30 of a single year.

projectScope.summary:
This project is for the construction and operation of a 1,439 kW Biomass-Fueled District Energy System (DES) in the City of Dawson. The Project is located within the Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in (TH) and the municipal boundary of the City of Dawson. The project area is zoned Downtown Core under the City of Dawson Official Community Plan and overlaps with Settlement Land parcels TH C-63FS/D, TH C-20FS/D, TH C-21 FS/D, TH C-35FS/D and TH C-26FS/D, as well as TH-owned land, freehold land, and Dawson City-owned land.
Project activities include decommissioning of an existing building, construction of a biomass boiler plant, installation of underground hydronic pipes to distribute heated propylene glycol, and operation of the DES. The expected lifespan of the DES is 20 years. Construction is anticipated to take up to 15 months, occurring between summer 2025 and March 31, 2027.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of Quartz Exploration operations on 2 797 claims known as the Rau Property. The exploration project was most recently assessed under YESAB Project 2014-0052 and subsequently was given their mining license in 2019 under LQ00531. The Project is located approximately 88 km northeast of the Village of Mayo and 41 km northeast from Keno City, within the Territory of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. The Project will be accessed by air via the Rau and/or Rackla (Orion) Airstrips. Exploration activities are proposed between May 1 and October 31, annually, while site maintenance, equipment delivery, and low-impact environmental studies may occur between November 1 and April 31, for a period of 10 years.

projectScope.summary: Yukon Government has been operating a small sewage treatment lagoon at a former gravel pit located off the Dempster Highway at km 3.9. The lagoon receives approximately 5 500 m3 of septic waste annually that is pumped from holding tanks, septic tanks, and outhouses at residences and government facilities in the Dawson area. In addition, sludge from the Dawson Wastewater Treatment Plant. Yukon Government intends to continue operating the Dempster Sewage Lagoon, with modifications to operational procedures, until a new wastewater facility is built in Dawson.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a placer mine along 136 claims and one placer lease (to be converted to 10 claims) along Black Hills Creek and its tributaries located approximately 66 km southeast of Dawson City. The Project is within the Territories of Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun and the Asserted Territory of White River First Nation. The Project falls within Goldfields Land Management Unit 11 (Integrated Stewardship Area 4) of the Dawson Recommended Land Use Plan. The Project area will be accessed by an existing network of off-claim roads, and newly constructed roads and trails. Activities are proposed to occur between April 15 and October 15, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is geotechnical investigations at three separate project locations: Kwanlin Dün First Nation Settlement Land parcels KDFN C-177B (Takhini Hotsprings Road), KDFN C-64B/D (South Klondike Highway and Cowley Creek) and KDFN R 77B (Alaska Highway, M’Clintock River). Project activities will inform the feasibility of future residential lot and/or subdivision development at the project locations. The Project falls within the Traditional Territories of Kwanlin Dün First Nation, Ta’an Kwäch’än Council and Carcross/Tagish First Nation. Project activities are anticipated to occur between winter 2024 to summer 2025, for a duration of approximately 25 days.

projectScope.summary:
The project consists of highway repairs and the installation of erosion prevention measures at km 1374 of the Alaska Highway. The purpose of the project is to resolve the current highway damage and prevent future damage caused by wave action. The project is located within the Marsh Lake Local Area Planning Boundary, approximately 42 km south of downtown Whitehorse. The Project falls within the Traditional Territories of the Carcross/Tagish First Nation, and the Kwanlin Dün First Nation, and involves using Kwanlin Dün First Nation Settlement Land parcel S-206 for access. 
Project activities will take three to four months to complete, beginning in either April 2026 or 2027. The proponent anticipates the project will be completed within five years (which accommodates potential delays from operational and environmental considerations, and timing of funding approval).

The Project is located within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory. The Project is approximately 17 km south of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and will be accessed by new and existing roads. Project activities are proposed to occur from April 1 to October 15, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is two adjacent placer mining operations in the Nordenskiold River Watershed:

Operation 1: 27 claims located on a tributary of Rowlinson Creek (UNLLT of Rowlinson Creek)
Operation 2: 24 claims located on a first-order tributary of Rowlinson Creek (Miller Creek) and a second-order tributary of Rowlinson Creek (UNLLT of Miller Creek)

These two operations by the same Proponent have been combined into a single assessment due to proximity and yearly temporal overlap. The Project is located approximately 20 km west of Carmacks in the Territory of the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation. No mining activities will occur within 60 m of adjacent Settlement Land parcel LSC S-86B1/D. Access to both operations is via Mount Nansen Road. Project activities will occur annually between April 1 and November 1 for ten years.

projectScope.summary: The purpose of the proposed project is to construct a new bulk fuel plant and office building at 153 Industrial Rd in Whitehorse where North 60 Petro currently operates a shop/storage building and storage yard. The proposed project includes building a new bulk fuel plant consisting of seven - 1 000 000 Liter (L) tanks, and five - 200 000 L tanks for a total storage capacity of 8 000 000 L. The bulk fuel storage would contain diesel, gasoline and jet fuel. The bulk fuel plant will be constructed within a secondary containment consisting of a lined and bermed facility. The Project is located in the City of Whitehorse within the Traditional Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta’an Kwäch’än Council. Project activities will begin in Autumn 2025 or Spring 2026, with construction taking approximately eight months to complete.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the development of land for residential purposes and new access. The proposed residential lot is approximately 3.01 ha and is located at km ~1586.5 on the Alaska Highway, ~2 km southeast of the Nygren Subdivision and 8 km northwest of Haines Junction. The Project is located within the Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Territory, cornerwise across the Alaska Highway from a Kluane First Nation (KFN) Settlement Land parcel (KFN S-39B1) and adjacent to existing residential lots. The property will be accessed via a new access point and road from the Alaska Highway. Development and building activities are expected to take over five years and commence in 2027; the disposition of land is indefinite.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a placer mining operation on 27 contiguous claims along Ledge Creek and its tributaries, 1 Mile Pup and No-name Pup #1. Ledge Creek is a right limit tributary of Mayo Lake within the Mayo River Watershed. The claim block is located approximately 52 km straight-line distance northeast of Mayo and 32 km straight-line distance southeast of Keno, with existing access via the Mayo Lake Road to the edge of Mayo Lake, and an additional 30 km across the water to the mouth of Ledge Creek. The Project is located within the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun and completely overlaps with Settlement Land Parcel NND R-2A. Project activities will occur from May 1 to October 31, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continued operation and management, as well as the expansion of a hydrocarbon contaminated Land Treatment Facility (LTF) in Dawson City.  The facility is located within the Callison Industrial Subdivision, approximately 5 km east of Dawson City in the Traditional Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in.  The proposed reclamation activities are proposed over a one-week period during the month of August.  The construction of the new facility will occur over one month during the month of September. Operation and management of the facility will occur year-round.

projectScope.summary:
Yukon Energy Corporation (YEC) is proposing to conduct trail brushing and maintenance activities along 131 existing access trails connecting the Robert Campbell Highway to the YEC powerline easement (L178). The trailheads are located between km 417 and km 577.6 between the Village of Carmacks and the Town of Faro. Re-establishing these access trails is being proposed to undertake necessary inspection, refurbishment, repair and upgrade to the aging Carmacks/Faro transmission line. In addition, YEC is proposing to clear up to 0.058 hectares near the Pelly River within the Town of Faro in preparation for future re-routing of a portion of transmission lines L178 and L356.
The Project is located within the Territories of the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation (LSCFN), Liard First Nation (LFN) and Ross River Dena Council (RRDC). Nine existing access trails overlap with Settlement Land parcels (LSC R-45B, LSC R-58B, and LSC R-56B), seven of which require land use authorization from LSCFN. Activities are proposed during the fall, winter, and spring seasons over one year from permitting. Trail brushing is expected to take approximately 8 weeks and clearing for the transmission line re-routing is expected to take up to 3 weeks. Maintenance activities are expected to continue for the life of the powerline.

records.title: Existing Powerline Access Trail Restoration - Carmacks / Faro Transmission Line (L178)

projectScope.summary: The Project is the exploration and development of 172 grants on Preacher Creek (including a UNLLT to Preacher Creek) and Iron Creek in the Stewart River Placer Watershed. The Project is located approximately 103 km south of Dawson City within the Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and Selkirk First Nation. Access to the project will occur via barge from Dawson City to the Thistle Creek barge landing (Yukon River), Barker Creek barge landing (Stewart River), and by air from Dawson City to the Thistle Creek and Barker Creek airstrips. Project activities are scheduled to occur from April 1 to November 1 annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the partial demolition and stabilization of the historic Canyon Creek Bridge. The Project is located 30 km east of Haines Junction, along the Alaska Highway. It is within the Territory of Champagne and Aishihik First Nations. Access to the work site is via the adjacent rest area and the old Kluane Wagon Trail. Work is scheduled to occur over a two-week period between August and November, and may commence as early as 2025, but is more likely to occur in the fall of 2026, 2027, or 2028, pending funding cycles.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 102 claims on Dominion Creek and tributaries in the Central Yukon – Indian River Watershed. The Project is within the Traditional Territories of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun, and within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Dawson Land Use Commission’s Recommended Plan. The project location is approximately 59 km southeast of Dawson City and will be accessed by the Goldfields road network, as well as new and existing on-claim access. Project activities are proposed to occur from March 15 to October 28, annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the development of a community-scale biodigester and gasification system to process municipal solid waste and converting it into thermal energy. The facility will be up to 1 000 sq feet and is designed to process up to 40 tons of organic waste annually, producing biofertilizer and biochar. Heat produced by the facility will be used to heat the biodigester, a greenhouse, and maintenance facilities at the Carmacks solid waste facility. Construction activities are planned to commence as soon as the required permitting is obtained. The facility is proposed to operate for up to 25 years, which is the lifespan of the equipment.
The Project is located within the municipal boundary of the Village of Carmacks, adjacent to the Carmacks solid waste facility, and within the Territory of the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation.

projectScope.summary:
The Project involves timber harvesting to provide a supply of sawlogs and fuelwood for local markets, including Yukon sawmill operations. Project activities will occur within the boundary of the Timber Harvest Plan (THP) area. Some harvesting and associated activities were previously assessed under YESAB Project 2018-0079. Project 2024-0096 will assess new activities based on the updated configuration of harvest operating units and associated roads/access within the 2024 Draft Marsh Lake Transfer Station THP.
The Project falls within the Traditional Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation, Ta’an Kwäch’än Council, and Carcross/Tagish First Nation. Timber harvesting and associated activities are proposed to occur year-round for a 15-year period ending in 2039. If natural regeneration does not meet reforestation stocking standards, silviculture activities may occur for an additional 10-year period after harvesting is complete.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the establishment and operation of a placer mine on 127 claims and 4-mile lease along Sestak Creek and 19 unnamed tributaries, in the Yukon River North Watershed. The Project is located approximately 66 km southwest of Dawson City, within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory and overlaps partially with the White River First Nation Asserted Territory. The Project falls within Land Management Unit 15: Khel Dëk (Sixty Mile) of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The Project will be accessed via barge and aircraft from Dawson City to the Sestak Creek barge landing and Sestak Creek airstrip respectively, and then by new and existing roads. Project activities are proposed to occur from April 15 to October 15 annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the establishment of a Class 3 placer mine on 11 claims along 11 Pup. The majority of the Project is in the Yukon River North Watershed, and a small extent is in the Stewart River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 75 km South of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory. Access to the Project will occur via the existing Henderson Dome and Maisy May Creek roads. Project activities will occur between April 1 to November 15 annually, for up to 10 years.

projectScope.activities:
Heavy Equipment Use
Clearing and Grubbing (3 750 m2)

Vegetation will be cleared on both sides of River Drive
Clearing will be done outside of bird nesting period (May 3 to August 20)

If clearing required during the bird nesting period, a biologist will complete a bird nest survey prior to clearing

Excavation, removal and disposal of roots, stumps, submerged logs and corduroy
Overburden stored at a maximum height of 1.5 m 
Stockpiles will be stored in the lots directly adjacent to River Drive (off the right-of-way), approximately 100 m from the Yukon River

Water Management

Drainage ditches and culverts will convey intermittent flows carrying precipitation and/or snow melt

Installation of 3 new culverts (64.75 m total)

Culvert(s) are approximately 450 mm in diameter 
Culvert to discharge water will be 15 m from Yukon River shoreline
Culvert to be fitted with flap gates to prevent reverse flow 

Work associated with drainage ditches and culverts will occur in the dry weather

Installation of temporary erosion control measures

Erosion control measures may include, but are not limited to, sediment fences, straw bales, poly cover for stockpiles or exposed roadway banks and erosion control matting


Road Construction

Approximately 7 300 m3 of pit run and 3 400 m2 of granular base course used for the project
Granular material, geotextile stabilization and/or rip rap may be required for slope stabilization
Granular material will be sourced from a local gravel pit

Imported granular material will get metal leaching/acid rock draining (ML/ARD) testing completed before use

Material not used immediately will be stockpiled at the staging area, which is < 30 m from the Yukon River

Dust Suppression

Dust control measures implemented based on visual inspections, community complaints, and during high dust generating activities 

Dust control measures to include water spraying as dust suppressant, speed limits in work area, daily inspections, and wheel wash stations for vehicles


Works Involving Existing Infrastructure

Boardwalk (50 m) removed prior to start of project and re-installed once project is completed
Gazebo Removed prior to start of project and re-installed once project is completed

Traffic Management 

Selected contractor to develop and implement a Traffic Management Plan 

The plan will take into consideration specific project activities, the site, public safety and worker safety

The contractor will work with the Village of Carmacks and Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation to communicate any temporary road closures 
Construction area on River Drive to be blocked with rope and/or barriers

Signage regarding the closure to be installed at either end of River Drive


Site Restoration

Site Restoration will occur once construction activities are completed 
Areas along the slope of the road graded, scarified and seeded with native seeds to encourage revegetation

Use of 3 600 m3 of topsoil/seed
All revegetation activities will be in accordance with the practices described in the Yukon Revegetation Manual


Fuel Storage and Handling 

Fuel will be supplied by truck mounted tidy tanks
A spill contingency plan will be posted on-site during the Project

Workers will be trained in the execution of the spill contingency plan and fuel handling

Spill kits will be on each piece of heavy equipment and at the staging area
Equipment will be kept in good condition and inspected daily 

Waste Management

Domestic solid waste stored in bear-proof bins until disposed of at local solid waste facility
One or more portable toilets will be at the staging area, waste from the portable toilet will be collected by a wastewater truck and disposed of at approved facility

Other

If possible, vegetation will be disturbed or removed outside of the bird nesting period (May 3 to August 20); If vegetation needs to be disturbed or removed within the bird nesting period a qualified professional biologist will carry out a bird survey. If nests are encountered, they will not be disturbed until juvenile birds have fledged
Areas where vegetation is removed/disturbed will be graded and reseeded to promote revegetation
Project activities will be carried out in accordance with the Government of Yukon guidelines Preferred Practices for works affecting Yukon Waters (2019)
Soil and/or imported gravel/rip rap materials will be stored more than 30 m from the Yukon River 
Rip rap material used for road slope and/or culvert stabilization will be washed and non-metal leaching and non-acid generating
Fuel storage and handling will occur a minimum of 30 m from the Yukon River
Spill kits will be present on each piece of heavy equipment and at the staging area
Areas where vegetation is removed and/or disturbed will be graded and reseeded to promote revegetation to reduce potential of erosion
The contractor will work with the Village of Carmacks and Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation to communicate the planned road upgrades, temporary road closure, and schedule to the community
Access to the Project location will be restricted with signage, barriers and caution tape 
Active construction areas will be left in a safe condition at the end of each workday (i.e. all equipment stored at staging area, signage posted indicating entrance to the site is restricted, no open excavations, no steep slopes, etc.); and if a worker see a member of the public approaching or within the Project area they will direct them away from the Project area.
projectScope.summary: The Project’s purpose is to upgrade a portion of River Drive, in the Village of Carmacks, to protect it from flooding. Upgrades include increasing the surface elevation of River Drive to approximately 120 mm above the 1:200-year flood event elevation. The Project takes place on Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation Territory and is adjacent to Settlement Land parcel LSC C-18FS. A former highways and public works property will be used as a staging area for materials and equipment. The Project is scheduled to occur between April 1 and October 31 of a given year, dependent on assessment timelines and development permit.

projectScope.summary: The Project involves the development of a fuel break and service roads, as well as selective timber harvesting to increase fire resistance. The Project is located within Teslin Tlingit Council (TTC) Territory, overlapping with multiple TTC Settlement Land parcels and lying within and to the north and west of the municipality of Teslin. The timber harvest area between the fuel break and the Alaska Highway corresponds with the Phase 2 treatment area of the Teslin Community Wildlife Protection Plan (CWPP). Project activities will take up to 15 years to complete, followed by ongoing maintenance.

The Project is located within the Traditional Territory of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation. Project activities are scheduled to occur over a two-week period in the fall season following permitting.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 159 claims and lease IW00870 on Canadian Creek, Britannia Creek and unnamed tributaries at the boundary of the Yukon River South Watershed and the White River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 140 km south of Dawson City and 100 km northwest of Minto, within the Territories of Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and Selkirk First Nation. The Project will be accessed by barge on the Yukon River from Minto or Dawson City or by air to an existing airstrip. The Project will take place from April 1 to November 15, annually, for 10 years.

The Project is road reconstruction for the purpose of installing a weigh-in-motion sensor system to support enforcement of highway weight restrictions. The Project is located at km 1410 of the Alaska Highway (approximately 1.5 km northwest of Wolf Creek), within the Traditional Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta’an Kwächän Council. Project activities are anticipated to begin in 2024, for a duration of approximately eight weeks.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the repair of Piers 3 and 4 of the Teslin River Bridge along the Alaska Highway at Johnsons Crossing, approximately 50 km northwest of Teslin. Bridge replacement activities will occur within the highway right-of-way and an existing bridgehead reservation (Disposition 105C06-055). The Project is located within the Territory of the Teslin Tlingit Council (TTC), directly adjacent to Settlement Land parcels TTC C-36B and TTC R-11B with access using existing roads under a specified access right through Settlement Land parcel TTC C-36B. Project activities will occur year-round over up to five years between July 2026 and 2035.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the renewal of a placer mine on 6 claims along Kimberly Creek in the Alsek River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 28.8 km northwest of Haines Junction in the Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Traditional Territory. The Project falls within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary and overlaps with CAFN Settlement Land parcel R-47A. There is no work proposed off-claim.  Access will be via an existing road that starts on the Alaska Highway and goes along the claims. Project activities will take place annually from May 1 to October 1, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing timber harvest and access development for six operating units within the Partridge West Timber Harvest area. The Project is located east of the North Klondike Highway between km 580 and 588, approximately 45 km northwest of Stewart Crossing. The Project is located in the Territories of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. The operating units will be accessed between August and April when ground conditions are such that rutting and compaction will not occur, through new access off of the Klondike Highway near km 586. Project activities will begin as soon as permits are obtained and will occur over 10 years. Silvicultural activities may occur for an additional 10 years based on regeneration assessments.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a grouped project of two individual placer mines on 225 claims along Sixty Mile River, Unnamed Right Limit Tributary (UNRLT) 5, and 12 Mile Creek in the Sixty Mile River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 48 km west of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 15 Khel Dëk (Sixty Mile) – Integrated Stewardship Area 3 of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in. The Project will be accessed via the existing Top of the World Highway, from Swede Creek Dome to the top end of California Creek, or from km 88 to the top end of the claim block along the Sixty Mile River. The Project will involve the creation of new roads, trails, and fords. Project activities are proposed to occur from March 1 to October 31, annually, for up to 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a placer mining operation on 82 claims on Ten Mile Creek and unnamed tributaries of the Five Mile Creek and Sixty Mile River within the Sixty Mile River watershed. The Project is located approximately 70 km upstream of Dawson City within the Traditional Territory of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in First Nation and within Land Management Unit 15: Khel Dëk (Sixty Mile) of the Dawson Planning Region. projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a mine on 32 claims along Parent Creek and its tributaries. The Project is located approximately 8 km southwest of Keno City, in the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. Project activities are scheduled to occur from April 15 to October 15, annually, for 10 years. projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 73 claims, along an Unnamed Right Limit Tributary (UNRLT), and its tributaries, of California Creek in the Sixty Mile Placer Watershed. The Project is located approximately 80 km west of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 15: Khel Dëk (Sixty Mile) of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and the Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in. Access to the Project will occur via the Top of the World Highway and three unnamed access roads. Project activities will occur between 01 May and 31 October, annually, for up to 10 years.

projectScope.activities:

Plots circular, 20 m in diameter (314.2 m2 each)
Plots to be marked with 1 m long wooden, or metal stakes hammered into the ground in the center of the plot

Consistent with protocol in the Field Manual for Describing Yukon Ecosystems 
Plant clippings taken from species that are unable to be identified in the field


Camp (for up to 7 people)

Campsite located near the mouth of the Nisutlin River preferably in an area already impacted by past camping activities (if this is not possible, minimal stripping of vegetation may be required)

Camp to be used for 5-7 consecutive days up to 3 times
A cook tent and the required number of sleeping tents will be used
Food stored in sealed containers
Camp surrounded by an electric fence 

Waste Management 

Latrine dug > 70 m away from river and filled at the end of the Project 
Dishwater disposed of in a sump dug > 30 m away from the river 

Only bio-degradable cleaning products used 

Garbage stored in sealed containers until packed out and disposed of at the Teslin landfill


Other 

No project activities to occur within Teslin Tlingit Council Settlement Land
Field crew will not knowingly disturb hunters or other recreationists
If an active bird nest is found at a plot site, the plot will be excluded from the sample and the next site will be located > 50 m away
If found, areas with invasive species will be documented; invasive species may be pulled and destroyed
All equipment cleaned prior to transport to the project area to avoid the introduction of invasive species
projectScope.summary:
The Canadian Wildlife Service is proposing activities related to a long-term vegetation monitoring program occurring within the Nisutlin River Delta National Wildlife Area, approximately 10 km northeast of the Village of Teslin. The monitoring program will aid in understanding habitat change within the delta and potential environmental and anthropogenic influences. Research will include determining the current composition of the vegetation communities and comparing the results with historic and future data. Permanent vegetation monitoring plots will be established to ensure consistency in future monitoring. Project activities will occur over 5 to 7 consecutive days between July 1 and September 15, up to 3 times between 2024 and 2028.
The Project is in located the Traditional Territory of the Teslin Tlingit Council.

projectScope.activities:
Heavy equipment use

All machinery will work above the high-water mark; no machinery will be stored overnight within 30 m of any surface water
All machinery will be cleared of vegetation and soil before being moved to or off the project area to prevent the spread of invasive species
If heavy machinery is required, only light low ground pressure (tracked) machinery will be permitted to operate in wetland areas while constructing project boardwalks 

Construction fencing of active construction areas
Fencing and signage of construction laydown area
Improving trails (2 500 m) and six entry point trails (combined length of 200 m)

Clearing between 1 600 m² and 2 800 m² to achieve an average width of 3 m

Re-route trail where necessary
Clearing outside the main bird nesting period of May 3 to August 20 for the area; if clearing required during this time, a qualified person will check for active nests and if any are found, buffers will be put in place until the young have fledged
Only trees required to be cleared for the trail route will be cleared; all other mature trees will remain in place

Up to 1 200 m of constructed/improved boardwalk

Boardwalks to be elevated above grade using posts or dunnage to minimize disturbance to riparian areas, lessen compaction of the riparian forest floor, and prevent visitors from stepping off boardwalks and into sensitive riparian areas
Areas of the south shore may also include rip rap placed in the waterbody to shore up areas where boardwalk is unfeasible and paving is required
Only clean rip rap with non-ARD/ML potential will be used
Rip rap will be placed individually into the lake (not end-dumped)
All wood products used for boardwalk construction will adhere to the best management practices for the use of preserved wood in aquatic and sensitive environments 

Construction and improvement of up to 1 600 m of packed earth and gravel trails and access point trails 

Approximately 900 m3 of granular material from an approved/permitted off-site source (non-ARD and non-metal leaching) applied to the area to be paved
Trail surfaces to be built to accessible grade and covered with asphalt, pavement, or a similar surface


Instream works during construction of boardwalk along south shore of Wye Lake

Turbidity curtains installed around in-water work areas prior to the placement of rip rap in the waterbody
Water quality during instream works to adhere to CCME Canadian Water Quality Guidelines for the Protection of Aquatic Life
Instream works to be monitored by a Qualified Environmental Professional 
Instream works will adhere to the Preferred Practices for Works Affecting Yukon Water) guidelines 

Upgrading timber footbridges, viewing platforms, and other infrastructure
Additional improvements to the park including:

Two trailhead kiosks with maps
Formalize trailheads and entry points
Replacement of the existing outhouse with an accessible outhouse
Upgrade bench and seating areas
New picnic area
Upgrade waste and recycling areas
Upgrade interpretive signage
New bicycle racks

On-site re-fueling and fuel storage

Pick-up truck with a mounted tidy tank; jerry cans transported via UTVs for re-fueling equipment on site
All re-fueling activities occur over containment trays (drip pans)
All stationary machinery will have containment trays placed under them
Spill response plan to be prepared by the contractor and approved by the Proponent
Spill clean-up kits to be available on-site
Fuel will be removed from site daily 

Other

Should heritage resources or archaeological sites be encountered, work shall stop immediately and the heritage branch of Liard First Nation and the Department of Tourism and Culture, Heritage Resources branch will be contacted to address the findings and provide guidance on next steps
A QEP will be present for all in-water work and for works overlapping wetlands in the north and west areas of Wye Lake Park; duties will include surveying for the presence of Western Toad and ensuring project works do not kill or injure any Western Toad 
Project activities will be paused if Western Toads are observed or tadpoles are encountered in the wetland ponds found throughout the project area; all observations of Western Toads will be reported to the Yukon Conservation Data Center
Riparian areas to be impacted by project activities will be surveyed by a qualified ecologist/botanist prior to the start of project activities to investigate for the presence of Slender Naiad
If Slender Naiad is observed to overlap with the footprint of the improved boardwalks, a dialogue will be initiated between the Proponent, the Yukon Conservation Data Centre, and Environment Yukon to determine the most suitable route for the improved boardwalk to minimize potential disturbance or destruction of this plant
The Proponent will conduct a fish habitat assessment for Wye Lake in the winter/spring (of 2025) and if warranted, acceptable avoidance and mitigation measures will be established during the process of a request for project review or Fisheries Act Authorization application with DFO
Excavated materials and debris will be disposed of above the high-water mark of Wye Lake and located such that they do not enter the waterbody

Project activities will take place from May to October over three consecutive construction seasons and start as early as spring/summer 2025 through fall 2030 at the latest.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a fuelbreak (i.e. a wide strip of land on which vegetation has been modified/cleared to mitigate fire spread) immediately southeast of the municipal boundary of the City of Whitehorse and will connect to the existing Whitehorse South fuelbreak to the west and will continue east to the South Klondike Highway. The fuelbreak will aid in wildland fire suppression, improve safety for access/egress of emergency response personnel, and establish potential control locations for containing wildland fire along the southern portion of the City of Whitehorse. The Project occurs within the Traditional Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta’an Kwächän Council. Project activities will occur seasonally from April 15 to December 1 each year. Project activities are proposed to occur from the summer of 2025 and continue into 2032.

decisionBodies: YG EMR Land Management Branch, Client Services

projectScope.activities:
Project Scope was updated October 23, 2025 - See bolded area
Access and Site Preparation

Access via existing right-of-way at the intersection with the Klondike Highway or Hunker Creek Road

Operating construction vehicles restricted to existing roads or established right-of-way

Site preparation will include: 

Clearing and mulching approximately 6 m wide and up to 3.6 hectares of vegetation within the YEC right-of-way for the construction of temporary access and egress routes in steep terrain 

Clearing of vegetation during the winter construction window 
If vegetation clearing must occur within the nesting period, pre-disturbance nesting surveys will be conducted by experienced avian biologists.

Potential rock removal 
Snow clearing

Flattening snow drifts, spreading plowed snowpiles, or safe removal and disposal of excess snow to an offsite location



Heavy Equipment Use

Use of a snowcat/bulldozer (D4 or D6 equivalent), a mulcher, excavators, floats and equipment movers, a tracked horizontal directional drill rig, and a tandem water truck
Staging for drilling activities will take place on dry land outside of the riparian zone or will be completed in frozen ground conditions with swamp matting on staging areas in the bounds of the YEC right-of-way

Installation of the Fibre Optic Transmission Line

Installation of an 8 km fibre optic line using horizontal directional drilling (HDD):

Fibre will be housed in a 32 mm (1¼ inch) high-density polyethylene (HDPE) conduit
Installed in approximately 200 m sections 
Target installation depth is 1 m below ground and 3 – 6 m below frozen watercourses
Drill alignment will parallel the YEC powerline at a 15 m offset
Fibre cable will be installed in the conduit using high-pressure air jetting
Bell holes (3 m x 4 m x 2 m) and drilling waste sumps (2 m x 3 m x 1 m) will be constructed at each drill entry and exit point
Drilling will be staged in the YEC right-of-way on dry land outside riparian zones or on frozen ground using swamp matting
Installation of six above-ground fibre pedestals (73 in by 32 in by 16 in) along the line 


Water Use

Water acquired from municipal source for directional drilling
Less than 100 m3/day
Mixed with clay mineral, Bentonite (3%)
Water collected in small onsite sumps

Sumps will have a maximum bore distance of 200 m and occur every 200 m within the existing right of way
Sump locations will be selected based on ground conditions during construction
Water will be mixed with bentonite to create a drilling mud slurry which will be mixed with excavated soil from the sump
Sumps will be capped using onsite soils


Fuel Use

Use of gasoline and diesel for drills, vehicles and other equipment
No bulk fuel is intended to be stored onsite

Only fuel within equipment and slip tanks within pickups for refueling equipment
Up to three slip tanks containing 900 L of diesel each

Refueling will be conducted at least 100 m from any watercourse
Equipment will be stored with drip trays; spill kits will be available in all equipment and vehicles.

Erosion and Sedimentation Control

Pre-construction inspection will be conducted to identify and address potential erosion and sedimentation risks
All project activities limited to frozen ground conditions
Use of silt fences and sediment traps or sump
An Environmental Monitor will conduct turbidity monitoring at water crossings, halting work and implementing corrective action if turbidity exceeds expected standards
Salvaged topsoil will be stockpiled at least 30 m from watercourses and reused for backfilling

Personnel

No camp
Up to 10 personnel on-site
Hotel and/or local accommodations planned

Waste Management

Food waste, refuse, and other attractants are securely contained in enclosed and approved bear-proof containers and/or facilities
Waste disposed of at an approved facility

Traffic Control

Flagging, signage, and speed reduction when working along road edge

Progressive Reclamation

Progressive reclamation-cleanup to occur immediately behind placement of cable and conduit installations and will include:

Backfilling of all excavations
Re-distribution of soils
Installation of water bars in sloped terrain (greater than 5% slope) to reduce potential erosion
Removal of equipment and excess materials/refuse 
All exposed soils will be reseeded with a certified weed-free native plant mix or mulched as necessary immediately following construction
Post-construction environmental site inspections are planned for summer 2026 to assess vegetation regrowth, identify any signs of erosion or sloughing, and confirm no adverse impacts to watercourses; a follow-up inspection in 2027 may be warranted based on 2026 findings.

Installation of silt fencing (where required) for sediment control in spring freshet



Other Considerations

Wildlife Management

All personnel will be trained on wildlife encounter avoidance and required to yield the right-of-way to wildlife
A maximum speed limit of 30 km/h will be enforced on project vehicles and equipment to reduce collision risk and disturbance
Construction activities will be minimized in high-value winter moose habitat and avoided whenever possible
Pre-construction wildlife surveys will be conducted to identify habitat features and apply appropriate setbacks
Clearing of vegetation to be completed at or near ground level to avoid injury to wildlife from stump splinters
Ongoing monitoring will be conducted by environmental and Indigenous monitors

Local Indigenous monitors will be hired to oversee project activities and assist in wildlife habitat conservation and disturbance mitigation

Work will be carried out during seasonal daylight hours, and any lighting required will be focused on the worksite to limit disturbance
Activities will be managed to avoid prolonged work in known wildlife corridors and to minimize noise and visual impacts in documented moose habitat
Bear awareness training will be provided to all personnel, including safe practices to avoid encounters, proper handling and use of approved deterrents, and methods to prevent bears from habituating to humans

Other Users

Consultation with local communities and Indigenous governments, as well as Department of Highways and Public Works have been initiated. Ongoing correspondence with Trondëk Hwëch’in is occurring to determine mitigation strategies for any concerns or impedances
Prior to the start of construction, the intention, rationale, and timeline of project activities will be communicated to resource users, including Trondëk Hwëch’in and recreationalists
All required permits, authorizations, and agreements, including those related to access and right-of-way establishment, and to obtaining free, prior, and informed consent where applicable will be secured
Use of temporary sound barriers or limiting construction to regular work hours to minimize sound disturbances when working near land parcels or residential areas 
Maintenance of user access and egress around active construction areas using signage and clearly marked alternate routes
In agricultural land zones, project activities will follow practices to preserve soil horizons and minimize compaction

Heritage Resources

If a suspected find is encountered, work will be halted within a 35 m radius and the area secured to prevent disturbance
On-site personnel will be informed of the procedure and its significance, ensuring that all suspected materials remain undisturbed
The Site Supervisor, Environmental Monitor, or a qualified archaeologist will notify the Yukon Government Heritage Resources Unit, Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in, and other relevant Indigenous communities or agencies as required
Reasonable measures will be taken to protect the site from unauthorized interference until official oversight is established
projectScope.summary: The Project is the relocation and installation of 8 km of fibre optic transmission line that heads southwest from the Klondike Highway approximately 26 km east of Dawson City to Hunker Creek Road. The transmission line will be constructed along an existing Yukon Energy Corporation power line right-of-way. Project activities occur within the Trondëk Hwëch’in Territory and on Settlement Land parcel TH S-166B1. The Project is located within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields and Land Management Unit 12: Tr’ondëk Täk’it (Klondike Valley). Construction is proposed to occur during winter months (December 2025 – March 2026) and is expected to take approximately three months.

Each camp is decommissioned during its final year of use
Camp 2 is secured prior to each seasonal shutdown

All waste removed from site
Drip trays placed below heavy equipment
Propane, oils and lubricants secured in locked enclosures
Fuel tank levels monitored to ensure no fuel or only small volumes of fuel remain on site


Water Use

Up to 299 m3/day from Evelyn and Murphy creeks for bridge replacement and camp activities
DFO’s end-of-pipe fish screen guidelines will be followed
If vegetation removed to place water pumps, sedimentation and erosion control measures will be implemented and the area will be reclaimed

Heavy Equipment Use

Equipment staging/storage and maintenance areas will be established at km 3 on the Canol Road (location of camp)

Storage or maintenance areas will be located at least 30 m from any watercourse

Heavy equipment inspected and cleaned before being transported to site and before being moved to new sites

Fuel Use and Storage

Up to 60 000 L diesel

Up to 2 storage tanks
1 B train fuel truck may be brought on site to refill tanks

Up to 15 000 L gasoline

1 storage tank
Pickup-mounted tidy tanks

Up to 3 000 L propane
Up to 5 000 L oils and lubricants
Spill kits on all equipment and at each water pump
Spill Contingency Plan on site
Refuelling from tidy tanks over drip pans 
Refuelling and storage at least 30 m from watercourses except

Water pumps located at least 2 m from watercourse in an impermeable liner/structure


Final Reclamation

At the bridge locations:

Recontouring and spreading of removed organics and overburden at temporary detours
Revegetation of the riparian zone outside the permanent footprint with native species

At the Canol Road gravel pits:

Recontouring and spreading of organic materials

At the camp:

Removal of all structures, equipment and materials
Removal of all sub-surface infrastructure
Removal of any spills in accordance with relocation permit
Recontouring and spreading of organic materials


Other

The Proponent commits to completing a heritage resources impact assessment over any areas of potential for heritage resources in advance of undertaking any project activities in these areas

All chance findings of heritage or archeological findings will be reported to the Heritage Department of Teslin Tlingit Council and the YG Heritage Resources Unit

Prior to project activities, a survey will be conducted by a professional biologist to identify woodchuck burrows and appropriate setbacks

A copy of the survey will be provided to Teslin Tlingit Council Lands and Resources Unit, Department of Lands, Resources and Heritage
Operators will be familiar with identification of woodchuck
Active woodchuck burrows will be avoided
Work will be halted if a woodchuck is observed until the animal leaves the area on its own

Personnel will be informed of the potential presence of bank swallows at the km 58.7 gravel pit

A QEP will assess the project area for nests prior to clearing and gravel extraction and appropriate buffers will be implemented
Piles of cleared materials will be sloped to 70 degrees or less to discourage nesting

Prior to granular resource extraction at the km 58.7 granular pit, a QEP will assess the location for leafy thistle

If leafy thistle plants are identified, an appropriate buffer will be flagged within which works will be avoided

Plans and timing of activities will be communicated to claimholders with placer mine claims that overlap or lie directly adjacent to a project site
Cleared coniferous timber with a diameter > 150 mm will be stockpiled in the road right-of-way for public salvage

Attractants will be stored in bear-proof containers; food scraps and waste will be packed out in odour-proof bags; human waste will be disposed of in catholes at least 50 m away from any water source; toilet paper will be burned or packed out
Cooking, food storage, and sleeping areas will be separated by at least 50 m in a triangle setup
All guides and clients will carry bear spray
Clients will be taken to the Da Kų Cultural Center and/or the Thechàl Dhâl' Visitor Centre for First Nations cultural interpretation
projectScope.summary: The Project is the operation of commercial, guided multi-day backpacking trips within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR). The Project is located within Territories of the Kluane First Nation and the White River First Nation. Trips are schedules to occur between July and September. The Proponent will operate annually for five years, starting in 2026.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the operation of commercial guided river trips and associated activities along the Alsek River, within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR). Trips start near Haines Junction at Serpentine Creek or the Dezadeash River bridge and end at Lowell Lake, Turnback Canyon or Dry Bay. Project activities occur within the Traditional Territory of Champagne and Aishihik First Nations. The Proponent will operate annually for five years, running multi-day trips between June and August.

projectScope.summary: The Project is an application for a 1.8 ha browsing lease that will be connected to an existing agricultural lot located on the Takhini Hot Springs Road, approximately 20 km north of downtown Whitehorse. The Project is located within the Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation (KDFN) and the Ta’an Kwächän Council, directly opposite the KDFN Settlement Land Parcel C-177B. Fencing, a livestock shelter, and an irrigation pond are proposed to be installed within April and July, following permitting; the proposed lease may be in place for up to 10 years.

projectScope.summary: he Project is a placer mine on 57 claims along Wolverine Creek, and five tributaries, in the White River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 50 km Northwest of Burwash Landing in the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation Territories. The Project falls within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary and is adjacent to Asi Keyi National Environment Park. Access will be via helicopter during the operating season, and winter access along the Donjek Road for equipment mobilization and supply. Winter access will occur between February 1 to April 30 and operation activities will take place from May 1 to October 15, annually, for 10 years.

indigenousGovernments: First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun

projectScope.activities:
Use of Heavy Equipment

Temporary Access and Site Preparation
Clearing within the right-of-way for equipment access
Construction of temporary leveled work areas for safe drill operation 
Grubbing activities to remove vegetative mat for access
The Contractor will avoid placing stockpiles within 10 m from riparian areas or watercourses
Hand tools (e.g., chainsaws) will be used to clear or remove vegetation within 10 m of the natural boundary of any waterbody or watercourse
Following completion, disturbed areas will be recontoured and reclaimed

Geotechnical Drilling

Use of sonic, auger, vibratory, rotary, mud rotary, or diamond drill rigs (truck- or track-mounted)
Up to 500 boreholes annually with up to 7 500 boreholes for the life of the Project
Boreholes up to 30 cm in diameter and 100 m in depth
Boreholes will be 200 km apart with up to 5 boreholes/km; up to 20 boreholes/km for bridge and culvert locations
Up to three drill rigs operating concurrently throughout the project area
Drilling may occur up to the ordinary high-water mark, where required to assess bridge or culvert infrastructure
Boreholes will be located at least 2 m away from underground utilities and a 7 m setback distance from overhead powerlines
Boreholes will be backfilled immediately after completion to prevent entrapment or injury to wildlife

Water use

Up to 100 m3/day sourced from streams, waterbodies and municipal systems

For purposes of:

Up to 60 m3/day for drill rig operation; and
General site water management i.e. dust control


Water pumps will be located at a minimum of 2m from the ordinary high-water mark

Placed within secondary containment capable of holding 100% of the pump's fluid capacity

All water pump intakes will include fish screens

Fuel and Equipment Management

Diesel: up to 5 000 L (one storage tank; up to two fuel trucks and tidy tanks)
Gasoline: up to 1 000 L (1 storage tank and tidy tanks)
Propane: up to 1 000 L 
Oils and lubricants: up to 500 L
Refuelling will occur over containment trays > 30 m of a watercourse
Water pumps refuelled using jerry cans or tidy tanks from the back of pickup trucks and a spill kit will be kept beside all water pumps
Spill kits and fire extinguishers will be located at stationary fuel storage areas and within mobile equipment
Equipment staging and fuel storage, if required, will occur within designated gravel pits or within the highway right-of-way in a manner that prevents release to the surrounding environment

Waste Management

No long-term storage of waste anticipated
All waste will be disposed of at an approved facility either in a community or in Whitehorse
Domestic waste will be stored within vehicles and disposed of daily at the accommodation site or at an approved disposal facility within nearby communities
For remote project locations, domestic waste will be stored in metal, wildlife-proof containers and disposed of at an approved facility at least once a week
Special waste (e.g., waste oil, lubricants) will be stored in airtight or steel locking containers

Traffic Management and Safety

The Contractor will prepare a Traffic Management Plan for review by HPW prior to starting work
All construction zones, speed limits, route markers and warning signs will be clearly posted
A notice of construction will be posted on Yukon 511 to inform the public of potential traffic interruptions

Accommodations

No camps proposed
Contractors will utilize commercial or private accommodations

Progressive Reclamation
Further Mitigations

Environmental Management Plan

Each Contractor is required to submit a project specific Environmental Management Plan (EMP) to HPW for review before the project starts
The EMP will outline environmental protection measures including but not limited to erosion and sediment control; fuel and spill management; contaminated soil testing, storage, removal or treatment procedures; water and wastewater use; water truck loading areas and pumping operations; procedures to test for and take leaking equipment out of service; garbage disposal and litter control; and protection of natural resources and wildlife
Erosion, Sedimentation and Contamination
Clearing will be completed in the winter or fall 
Erosion and sediment control measures (e.g., sediment fences) will be installed

Health and Safety for Workers

The Contractor shall prepare a Project Specific Safety Plan for review by HPW prior to starting the work

Heritage and Archaeological Resources

Prior to ground-disturbing activities, a Heritage Resource Overview Assessments will be reviewed or completed where none exist
Areas identified as known or newly discovered heritage resources will be avoided and flagged with a 30 m buffer, unless otherwise directed by the YG Heritage
projectScope.summary:
The Project is a territory-wide geotechnical drilling program within all Yukon highway rights-of-ways (ROW) listed in Schedule 1 of the Yukon Highways Regulations and associated bridgehead reserves under the jurisdiction of the Government of Yukon, Department of Highways and Public Works (HPW).
Activities will include soil and core sampling to characterize subsurface conditions in support of future activities such as highway and bridge design, repair, maintenance, and reconstruction (not included in this project.) Drilling will occur on existing road embankments or elsewhere within the right-of-way, including adjacent bridges and culverts. Project activities will occur year-round, for up to 15 years.
No work will take place on private land or Settlement Land.
To see the First Nation Territories this project overlaps with, please see the map package located on the YESAB Online Registry (YOR).

projectScope.activities:
Project Scope was updated January 12, 2026 - See bolded area
Access

Access to the project area is via the Alaska Highway and McLean Lake Road

Land Treatment Facility (LTF) Operation

General operations

Continued operations within the 15 ha land treatment facility leased area (9 ha currently undeveloped and 6 ha developed)
Accommodation of up to 70 000 m3 of contaminated materials
Berms and diversion ditches divert surface water around the LTF, while on-site meltwater and rainwater evaporate or infiltrate into the biopiles

Transport and acceptance of materials to the LTF

Transport of contaminated materials to LTF by private companies

Only materials authorized under a Yukon Government–issued relocation permit or covered under an environmental protection order in the case of an emergency cleanup, will be accepted

Transport of contaminated materials to LTF by Arctic Backhoe

Use of suitable cover to prevent release of contaminated soil during transport
Use of appropriate tanker truck to prevent release of contaminated liquid during transport
Review of laboratory analytical results to verify treatability prior to transport
Notification of Yukon Government as part of the relocation permit application process
Maintenance of records in accordance with regulatory requirements
Adherence to all terms of the relocation permit and/or environmental protection order


Contaminated soil deposition and treatment

Soil treatment area is currently designed to accommodate a capacity of approximately 30 000 m3 of soil
Contaminated soil dumped into windrows and then tilled into biopiles up to 4 m high
A new windrow or separate soil stockpile is created for each new shipment of contaminated soil to prevent mixing of soils from different sites
Soil from smaller stockpiles mixed based on analytical results 

Mixed stockpiles will not exceed 500 m³ per stockpile

Emergency spill materials from the Whitehorse area may be temporarily stored in the soil treatment area, within a segregated zone, concrete cell, or above-ground storage tanks while contaminant testing is completed

Materials meeting Yukon Contaminated Sites Regulation Park land use standards will be removed without treatment

Spills or leaks with soil hydrocarbon concentrations greater than 30 000 ppm are treated as highly contaminated under the current operating permit

Highly contaminated soil placed in a concrete holding cell for pre-treatment
Holding cells are sloped to drain liquid through a valved screening mechanism into underground liquid containment tanks
Sloped concrete pads have a maximum capacity of approximately 125 m³ at 85% full
Pre-treatment methods include frequent tilling, addition of nitrogen-based fertilizer and use of modified biopile system
Once analytical results show the soil is no longer highly contaminated, it is relocated to the soil treatment area

Natural remediation is used to treat hydrocarbon-contaminated soils, typically completing within two to five years

Biopiles regularly tilled (typically bimonthly) to promote biodegradation

Soils that have been treated, sampled, and approved for reuse will be stockpiled within the lease area (LTF and/or quarry) 

Sump waste deposition and treatment

Sump waste includes waste from garage sumps, wash bays, and city storm sewers and not intended to contain human waste
Sludge from sump waste is placed into the lagoons
Once the holding cell or liquid containment tank reaches capacity, its contents are batch-tested before being transferred to biopiles or shipped to an approved facility, depending on metal concentrations

Contaminated snow deposition and treatment 

Contaminated snow or ice is placed in a holding cell or in one of two lagoons (each <600 m2)
During spring, contaminated snowmelt is collected in liquid containment tanks connected to the holding cell or directly into a lagoon
Contaminated meltwater is sprayed onto biopiles as required, and contamination is remediated along with the soil in the biopile

Contaminated liquid deposition and treatment

Liquids are pumped into one of nine above-ground storage tanks or into one of two lagoons for storage

Temporary storage tanks range in capacity from 19 000 L (5 000 gal) to 78 000 L (20 000 gal), with a total capacity of 341 000 L (90 000 gal)

Each shipment of contaminated liquid is stored separately
If analytical results show liquids do not exceed CSR Protection of Aquatic Life Standards, they may be sprayed onto biopiles or pumped into a liquid holding cell

If contaminated liquids are sprayed onto biopiles, they are remediated with contaminated soil
If liquids are found to be unacceptable for treatment (e.g., highly contaminated with metals), they are shipped to a licensed facility outside the Yukon for treatment


Water use

Up to 100 m3 of water per year from municipal sources, transported and stored in steam trucks, and used to steam clean on-site tanks and spray biopiles

On-site tanks for recycling/reuse

Storage or reuse of up to 30 tanks from the remediation of contaminated sites (e.g., household underground storage tanks)
Cleaned prior to arrival or cleaned at the LTF 
Cleaned tanks may be transported to spill sites and used to collect contaminated liquids for treatment at the LTF
Stored up to three years or recycled at a facility outside of Yukon

Monitoring and testing

Sampling and requesting laboratory analyses of soil, snow, liquid, and sludge before, during, and after the remediation process
Inspecting, maintaining, and testing the compacted glacial till silt liner

The liner is tested after each biopile is remediated and removed from the treatment cell to confirm it is not contaminated.
Any contaminated liner material within the facility is excavated and treated in a biopile to meet CSR Park Land use standards

Excavated areas are refilled with compacted glacial till silt to repair the liner 


Testing water quality in existing monitoring wells on-site

Groundwater sampling is conducted twice a year


LTF expansion

Expansion of the LTF within the current lease boundary to accommodate future soil treatment needs 

Clearing of up to 9 ha, with trees piled and burned on site or salvaged for fuel wood (≥10 cm)
A compacted glacial till silt liner will be added to the cleared area
Vegetation clearing will occur outside of the bird nesting season (May 1 to July 31)
 If clearing must occur within the migratory bird nesting season, then nest surveys will be completed by a qualified biologist prior to clearing 


Closure and decommissioning

Closure and decommissioning are not included in the current project scope; Any future decision to close the facility would be made jointly by Arctic Backhoe and the Yukon Government, in accordance with Permit 24-002 and applicable Yukon Government protocols, including soil sampling and reporting requirements


Quarry Operation

Production of 3 000 to 10 000 m3/year of material (including pit run, crushed basecourse, bedding sand, bedding stone, blast rock, absorption field drain) 
Pit faces in active areas will typically be up to 5 m in depth
On-site crushing

Twice per/year, for periods of up to three weeks 
7:00 a.m. to 11:00 p.m. 

Routine activities such as excavation, screening, hauling, and sorting will take place seasonally as needed

Excavation hours of operations: 7:00 am to 11:00 pm 

Use of a diesel-powered mobile screening plant to sort and stockpile materials periodically based on demand

Operates from a Power Van and situated on a foundation of compacted pit floor
Sand stockpiles up to 1 000 m3 
 Crushed material up to 3 000 m3 

Sorting oversized rocks
Occasional blasting and sorting of rock
Transport of excavated materials to construction sites based on demand
Reclamation

Periodic review of the development plan to identify areas no longer required for extraction or storage

Recontouring of inactive areas to match surrounding terrain, with final slopes no steeper than 3:1
Redistribution of usable overburden from existing stripping stockpiles to promote natural revegetation
Decommissioning of unneeded access roads through construction of berms or water bars, as appropriate

Consultation with Yukon Lands Branch to confirm additional reclamation requirements, if any


Fuel Management

No stationary fuel storage on site 
Excavator refueled using a truck-mounted Tidy Tank (up to 600 L)
All other vehicles fueled at a commercial station in Whitehorse or elsewhere in Yukon
At least one spill kit carried on vehicles used for refueling at any given time

Personnel

Up to six employees, all of which are accommodated locally in the City of Whitehorse
The current lease allows for a caretaker at the site and currently, there is single accommodation on-site for this purpose

Waste Management 

All waste produced from the project would be managed in accordance with the Yukon Wildlife Act, to prevent attracting dangerous and nuisance wildlife, such as bears
No waste stored onsite; any waste that is generated during operations is to be removed from site and disposed of at the end of each workday 
Secure bin to dispose of waste at the caretaker residence 

Heavy Equipment Use
Other

Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat

Lagoons on site are constructed, with sloped berms and a shallow, flat floor, in a manner to allow for egress from the lagoon should wildlife enter

Aquatic Resources

The Proponent/contractor would at all times have on site sufficient spill cleanup equipment and materials in readiness to immediately contain and cleanup all deleterious substances which may be spilled and be familiar with spill protocols, as contained in the Spill Contingency Plan
Equipment would be cleaned of excess soil from buckets and tracks prior to being used in an area outside the LTF
Vehicles and equipment would be maintained in good working condition to minimize risk of spills or leaks
Where there is a potential for soil erosion, exposed soils will be revegetated with native species to enhance soil stability, and armouring or other techniques may be employed to mitigate soil erosion if required
Deleterious substances (including but not limited to fuels, lubricants, hydraulics, and coolants) of any type would not be deposited or allowed to be deposited into any waters, or in any place where the deleterious substances may enter any waters

Wetlands

Activities in wetlands will be avoided and no wetland disturbance will occur

Heritage Resources

If required, heritage resource assessment would be completed prior to clearing for continued LTF development
Should any artifacts or heritage resource items of significance be uncovered during project groundworks, these activities would be stopped immediately and TKC, KDFN, and YG Heritage Resources Branch would be notified. Groundworks would not resume until the items of significance had been recovered
No artifacts or objects would be removed from site by the contractor or other individuals, other than those permitted to do so

Other Users

Arctic Backhoe has a good working relationship with the quartz mineral claim holders and will communicate with the claim holders about any plans that the Proponent has for the Project
projectScope.summary:
The Project is the continued operation and maintenance of the Arctic Backhoe Ltd. land treatment facility and quarry on a lease of 28 ha.  The land treatment facility associated with the Project will continue receiving and remediating hydrocarbon contaminated soils, sludge, snow, and liquids in accordance with the Contaminated Sites Regulation. The quarry involves the extraction, screening, crushing, sorting, and hauling of aggregate materials.
Proposed activities will occur year round for approximately 40 years. The Project is located within the municipal boundaries of Whitehorse on McLean Lake Road, approximately 2.8 km from the Alaska Highway. It is within the Traditional Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta’an Kwäch’än Council.

records.title: McLean Lake Commercial Land Treatment Facility and Quarry
