The Project is located approximately 60 km northwest of the Village of Carmacks and will be accessed by the existing Freegold and Casino Roads. The Project is located within the Traditional Territories of the Selkirk First Nation and the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation. Project activities are scheduled to occur from March 1 to November 15, annually, for 10 years.

The project is the demolition of one building and two secondary structures located at 205 Rogers Street in downtown Whitehorse. One single-story building, a greenhouse, and a garage will be demolished, and the lot will be left leveled and graded. 
The project is on the traditional territory of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta'an Kwäch'än Council. Construction activities are set to occur during the snow-free months of 2024 or 2025 (contingent on contractor availability) and will be completed by fall.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is an amendment to an existing class 4 placer mining operation on 93 claims on Black Hills Creek and its tributaries within the Stewart River Watershed, previously assessed by the Designated Office under YESAB Project 2024-0165. This amendment includes the addition of the Unnamed Right Limit Tributary 2 (UNRLT 2) to be added as a water acquisition location. 
The Project is located approximately 94 km southeast of Dawson City and is located within the Territories of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyӓk Dun and Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in. Amended project activities are proposed from April 15 to October 15 annually, for the duration of the water licence.

Clearly communicated understanding of everyone’s roles and responsibilities
A commitment to safe work practices and procedures
Camp design and layout that ensures all personnel have access to private, safe accommodation
Conflict avoidance through open conversation and discussion, supported by a history of successful conflict resolution
Daily safety and planning meetings to provide a structured space for open discussion
Creation of a safe space where all personnel can feel comfortable noticing and addressing potential harassment, conflict, grudges, or violence issues before and when they arise, including acknowledgment of the potential for workplace violence or harassment and intent to create protections against such events

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the construction and operation of a shop and garage building and related infrastructures by the First Nation of the Na-Cho Nyäk Dun (FNNND). The purpose of the Project is to provide enclosed, heated storage for vehicles (including a vacuum truck), equipment, materials required to service water and sewer infrastructure within the community, and emergency maintenance materials.
The Project will take place on Lot C-6B-1 within the C6 subdivision, located on Settlement Land parcel NND C-6B near the Village of Mayo. The project site will be accessed using existing road network connected to Silver Trail. Construction activities are anticipated to start in May 2026 and end by December 2026, with construction hours from 7 a.m. to 7 p.m.

projectScope.summary:
The proposed project is to develop up to five granular pits along the Aishihik Road from km 42 to km 117, to provide gravel material for the future repair and maintenance of the road. The Project is within Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Territory, approximately 66 km northeast of Haines Junction. Settlement Land parcels CAFN R-36A and CAFN S-37B1 overlap with the proposed pits.
Test pitting and pit development will be phased over the next three to four years, with two pits being developed in 2025. Test pitting is proposed to be carried out in June/early summer; pit development is carried out in August for approximately three seasons. Operations would be up to 3 weeks per year, for the next 25 years. The season of operation will be between August 20 to October 31 and general operating hours will be from 7 am to 6 pm, 7 days a week.

This amendment includes the addition of up to three diversions for a total of up to 1 800 m of the Sixty Mile River to be diverted. The Project is located within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory and located approximately 47 km west of Dawson City. The Project falls within Land Management Units 15: Khel Dëk (Sixty Mile) and 21: Wëdzey Tąy (Fortymile Caribou Corridor) of the Final Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The Project will be accessed via the Top of the World Highway and by new and existing roads. Project activities are proposed to occur from March 1 to October 31 annually, for the remainder of the existing licence, 9 years.

Wildlife

All work will cease when moose are observed from the active claim block until the animals move off the project area on their own accord.
Prior to and during any vegetation clearing or ground-disturbing activities within the migratory bird nesting window, the project site will be actively monitored for signs of nesting activity by qualified personnel.
If active nests or bird activity indicating possible nesting are observed, all clearing and disruptive activities in the vicinity will cease immediately.
A no-disturbance buffer will be established around any confirmed active nests, consistent with best management practices and applicable federal and territorial guidelines and maintained until nesting is complete and the young have fledged.
Work may resume only once it is confirmed by a qualified observer that the area is no longer actively used for nesting.

projectScope.activities:

projectScope.summary: The Project involves reconstructing 10.9 km of the Takhini River Road, from km 0 to km 10.9, along with the entirety of Gully Road. The Project aims to improve the structural alignment, safety, and drainage of the roadways. It includes raising, widening and realigning these sections. The Project is located approximately 30 km northwest of downtown Whitehorse and is within the Territories of the Kwanlin Dün, Ta'an Kwäch'än Council and Champagne and Aishihik First Nations. The Project is near or adjacent to settlement land parcels TKC S-198B1, KDFN C-1B, TKC R-18B, KDFN S-365B1, and TKC R-13B. Water will be sourced from the Takhini River or via water truck from the municipal supply in Whitehorse. Gravel will be sourced from the North Klondike gravel pits at km 196.3 and 223.1, as well as from the Alaska Highway gravel pit at km 1438.7. These pits will be expanded to meet the project's granular resource needs. The Project falls within the Hot Spring Road Local Area Planning boundary and the Whitehorse YLUPC Planning Region. Reconstruction is planned to occur seasonally from May 1 to October 31, while clearing, geotechnical testing, and pit expansion may happen in any season. Work hours will be between 6:00 am and 7:00 pm, over the next 20 years, beginning in 2026.

New roads: ~ up to 2.3 km length by 8 m wide, with a 25 m road right-of-way

Road subgrade material will be sourced from onsite borrow pits; surface material will be developed (i.e. crushed and sieved) on site or sourced from an approved quarry nearby

New intersections: 

One new four-way intersection at the Klondike Highway/ Molison Drive location and Dredge Pond II east access; this will require realignment of the Molison Dr. access and decommissioning of the existing Molison Dr. access
One new T-intersection at the Klondike Highway and Dredge Pond II west access

New pedestrian crossings at key locations for safe access
New pedestrian trails
Signage, flagging, cones, and alternative routes established during construction

Site Preparation 

Clearing: up to 6 ha cleared of trees and shrubs, and grubbed of roots and debris

Salvageable wood will be stacked at an accessible location and available to the community
Tree clearing will occur outside the migratory bird nesting period for Northern Yukon (early May – late August); otherwise a qualified biologist will carry out a nesting bird survey prior to clearing
Brush will be mulched or disposed of at the Quigley Solid Waste Disposal Facility 
Organic materials stockpiled for future use

Excavation, sorting and crushing of gravel material from the site (i.e. borrow pit areas) to build roads, driveways, building pads and for pond infills

Borrow areas are the previously disturbed tailings piles, located above the 0.5% flood frequency flood elevation
Borrow pits will be established, used, and reclaimed incrementally 
Borrow pits will be located >200 m from sensitive receptors (i.e. residential areas)

Infill of 21 existing dredge ponds

Dewatering and filling of nearby tailings piles (i.e. site borrow pits) for infill
Two of the proposed ponds to be infilled have a hydraulic connection to the Klondike River (E35 and E36, total of .32 ha infilled)
Fish salvage to remove and relocate fish from infilled ponds

Fish Habitat Offsetting Plan:

Framework to enhance biodiversity, productivity, and functional fish habitat by increasing the connectivity of isolated ponds to each other and the Klondike River
The connections are anticipated to be open-air, ~ 1.0 m wide & 0.5 m deep shallow-water channels (at average water levels), or, if open-air is unfeasible, culvert connections

Installation of drainage ditches and culverts for management of stormwater runoff    
Installation of above ground utility services (power, luminaires and telecommunications) using power poles and lines (lines length: up to 2800 m) 
70% of the total project area (~105 ha) will be open spaces and a historic park 

Flood management and mitigation 

Development prohibited within a 200 m buffer from Klondike River
Detailed design and completed flood maps for the Klondike Valley (anticipated to be completed by YG in 2025) will confirm the appropriate freeboard height
Low-vulnerability accessory structures such as sheds, greenhouses, and landscaping features are permitted to be situated below this level

Water Use

100 m³/day for dust suppression acquired from Dawson Water Treatment Plant

Waste management during development:

Use of porta-potties or trailer washroom facilities (with pump out tanks) for workers
Hazardous waste disposed of at an approved facility
All other wastes disposed of at the Quigley Waste Facility
Attractants such as food, fuel and other waste will be stored in a bear proof containers until disposed; wildlife may be deterred from interacting with contractors by use of air horns or whistles

Fueling

Refueling equipment will occur in designated areas over 30 m from water with fueling trucks, with spill trays
No fuel storage onsite

No camp required

Up to 20 workers will be hired locally or accommodated locally in the City of Dawson

Residential Building Construction

Owners purchasing lots will be responsible to complete individual lot accesses (with culverts), minor drainage and grading, construct single detached dwellings and accessory buildings, and install associated on-site servicing (i.e. utilities, water and sewer) 
Septic holding tanks and pump outs will be required to manage residential sewage

Further Mitigations

If a chance encounter with heritage resources occurs during construction, work in the area will be stopped and YG and TH will be contacted
A spill prevention and response plan will be developed
A sediment and erosion control plan will be prepared before construction begins
A grading plan will be prepared during the detailed design
A traffic management plan will be developed before construction
A stormwater management plan will be developed during the detailed design
projectScope.summary: Government of Yukon Department of Community Services, Land Development Branch is proposing to develop the Dredge Pond II Subdivision located along the Klondike Highway, within the City of Dawson municipal boundary. The subdivision will include the site preparation of approximately 42 new residential lots (0.4 to 0.8 hectares each), installation of associated accesses and utilities, and flood control works. Flood control works will include a new diversion channel (for the Klondike River) and require fish habitat offsetting including pond modifications. The proposed project occurs within Trʼondëk Hwëchʼin Territory and within Land Management Unit 13: Ch’ë̀nyä̀ng (City of Dawson) - Community Area of the Dawson Recommended Land Use Plan. Project activities are anticipated to begin in 2025 for up to 7 years; land disposition is indefinite.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 49 claims along Brewer Creek, a tributary of the Stewart River. The Project is 100 km south of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Territory of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and the Asserted Territory of White River First Nation. Access to the project will occur by road from a barge landing on the Stewart River, or via road from an airstrip located between Brewer and Thetford Creek valleys. Claims in the project area are accessed by 4.5 km of existing roads, with up to 2 km of new roads developed for the project. Project activities will occur annually between May 15 to October 1 annually for up to 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is an amendment to an existing placer mine (PM17-053), previously assessed by the Dawson Designated Office in 2017 (YESAB project 2017-0045). The proposed amendment includes the addition of 68 placer claims for a total of 168 claims. The Project is located along the Sixty Mile River and Twelve Mile Creek in the Sixty Mile River watershed. It is 52 km west from Dawson City within the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Traditional Territory and falls within Land Management Unit 15: Khel Dëk (Sixty Mile), of the Dawson Planning Region. Project activities will take place March 1 to October 31, annually, until July 5, 2027.

projectScope.summary: The Project involves repairing the Lewes River Bridge, with a focus on the middle supporting concrete pier. A visual inspection conducted in 2023 revealed that the pier was in poor condition. The bridge is located at km 1393 of the Alaska Highway, approximately 12 km east of Whitehorse. It is located within the territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta'an Kwäch'än Council. Activities will take place underneath the bridge on the centre pier. Work is planned for spring, summer or fall (April to October) within the next five years, during periods of low water levels. The project is expected is to take place approximately three months to complete and will operate from 7:00 am to 7:00 pm, whenever possible.

The Project is located approximately 40 km from Stewart Crossing in the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. The Project will be accessed via the McQuesten River Road. projectScope.summary: The Project is a continuation of a placer mining operation on 213 contiguous claims on Duncan Creek, Beliveau Creek and Alyssa Pup, in the Mayo River Watershed. The Project is located within the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun Territory, approximately 36 km northeast of the Village of Mayo. The site will be accessed via the Silver Trail and Duncan Creek Road. Project activities are proposed to occur from March 15 to November 15, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the development of a commercial aggregate quarry along the Robert Campbell Highway, approximately 5 km north of Carmacks. The Project is located within an active public quarry and will be accessed by an existing access, which will be gated.  The Project is located in the Territory of the Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation (LCSFN), on Settlement Land parcel (SL) LSC-C23B. The quarry will be utilized annually for 10 years following permitting, from May to November. General operating hours will be from 7 am to 6 pm, Monday to Friday.

projectScope.summary: The project is for placer mining on 15 claims along UNRLT of Montana Creek, in the Yukon River Watershed. Access to the site is by existing roads. The project is located approximately 55 km southeast of Dawson City, within the Trʼondëk Hwëchʼin Traditional Territory and the traditional territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. It falls within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields.  Project activities are proposed to take place from March 1 to November 1 annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a commercial timber harvest program to be conducted within Operating Unit H of the MacIntosh East Timber Harvest Plan area. The Project is located approximately 5 km north of Haines Junction within Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Territory. The Project will be accessed via Old Mackintosh Road (Raven View Road) Project activities are proposed to begin in the summer of 2026 and will occur over 10 years. Harvesting may occur between June 1 and September 30, and December 1 to April 30 annually unless environmental conditions do not allow operations. Road construction may occur between June and March. Reclamation and silvicultural activities may occur for an additional 10 years based on regeneration assessments, for a total temporal scope of up to 20 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 178 claims on Indian River, McKinnon Creek, and Unnamed Left Limit Tributaries (UNLLTs) to the Indian River, in the Indian River Watershed. The Project is within the Territories of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun, and within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Dawson Recommended Land Use Plan. The project location is approximately 38 km south of Dawson City. It will be accessed by the Goldfields road network, existing on-claim roads, and new and existing trails in the project area. Project activities are proposed to occur from March 1 to November 28, annually for 10 years.

Changes are in bold.

projectScope.summary:
The project is the construction of two pathways to Army Beach, from Army Beach Drive, at Marsh Lake. The purpose of the project is to provide community access to Army Beach; to prevent motorized vehicle use of pedestrian access routes to Army Beach; to limit the impacts to vegetation and ecology by establishing access points to Army Beach. The project occurs on Crown land reserve parcels where there are currently informal access trails. The pathways will be constructed of compacted/packed gravel and will include a gravel pad atop metal staircases that provide access to the beach. Construction is to occur in the summer of 2024, 2025 or 2026, over an estimated 12-week period (six weeks per pathway).
The project occurs within the Traditional Territories of Carcross/Tagish First Nation and Kwanlin Dün First Nation.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a class 4 placer mine on 3 claims along Hunker Creek in the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 20 km east of Dawson in the Traditional Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in. The Project falls within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The Project will be accessed by existing roads. Activities are proposed from April 1 to October 31, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 81 contiguous claims on Australian Hill and within the Klondike River and Hunker Creek valleys in the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is within the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory and falls within Land Management Units 11 Goldfields and 12 Tr’ondëk Täk’it (Klondike Valley) of the Dawson Land Use Commission’s Recommended Plan. Sections of the Project overlap with Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Category B Settlement Land Parcel S-126B1 and are adjacent to Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Category B Settlement Land Parcel C-7B. An existing out of stream reservoir overlaps with Settlement Land Parcel S-126B1 and no other mining activities are proposed to occur on this parcel. Some claims also overlap with the Klondike Highway, Hunker Road, and Dawson City Airport. The Project is approximately 13 km east of Dawson City and will be accessed by Hunker Road, as well as new and existing on-claim access. Project activities are proposed to occur from April 1 to November 1, annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing a completely heli-supported quartz mineral exploration program on the Rye Property, which consists of 28 claims approximately 160 km northeast of the community of Ross River. The Rye Property is located in southeastern Yukon near the Yukon/Northwest Territories (NWT) border and will be accessed via helicopter from a staging area at km 418.2 North Canol Road. Activities are scheduled to occur annually from June 1 to November 30, for up to 10 years. The Project is located within the Territories of the Ross River Dena Council (RRDC) and the Liard First Nation (LFN).

The Project is the extension of an existing road, near km post 1682 of the Alaska Highway, to access a cabin on Settlement Land (SL) parcel KFN S-53B1. The Project is located approximately 1.7 km from Destruction Bay, within the Traditional Territories of Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation. Project activities will start at the end of September, early October or as soon as permitting is in place. Road construction is expected to take up to 6 days. The use and maintenance of the access and SL developments are indefinite.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Arctic Wild, is proposing to conduct guided river trips along the Firth River. Guided trips of up to 16 people will occur over 10-14 days in June and July on an annual basis, for five years beginning in 2025.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing timber harvesting for ten Operating Units (OUs) near Silver City, within the Silver City Fuel Abatement Timber Harvest Plan (THP). The Project is located in the Territories of Kluane First Nation and Champagne and Aishihik First Nations, and on both sides of the Alaska Highway. Harvested OUs will occur >400 m from residential lots and do not overlap Settlement Land parcels. The OUs will be harvested year-round (predominantly over winter) except for SC-26, which will only be harvested in winter (December to April). Project activities are planned to begin in the fall of 2025 and will continue for 10 years. Silvicultural activities may occur for an additional 10 years based on regeneration assessments, for a total temporal scope of up to 20 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on five claims along Hunker Creek and Little Gem Gulch in the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located within the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Traditional Territory. The Project is approximately 27 km south-east of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and will be accessed by the Hunker Creek Road, and by existing and new roads in the project area. Project activities are proposed to occur from April 1 to November 15, annually, for 10 years. No activities are proposed on Settlement Land.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a Class 3 Placer Mine on six claims along an unnamed tributary of Mayo Lake, located within the Mayo River Watershed and approximately 18 km southeast of Keno, and 38 km northeast of Mayo in the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun (NND). The Project will be accessed by barge, travelling approximately 20 km across Mayo Lake. The existing barge landing is accessible from Mayo Lake Road, which branches off the Silver Trail Highway. Project activities will take place between May 1 and October 31 annually, for 5 years.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the establishment of a Class 4 placer mine, currently permitted under a Class 3 Licence, on three claims along Bonanza Creek, in the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 6.9 km south of Dawson City, within Dawson Land Management Unit 11- Goldfields. The Project is located within the Territory of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in. The Project will be accessed using the existing Bonanza Road and other existing trails. Project activities will occur from April 1 to November 1, annually, for 10 years.
*Project scope changes were made as a result of the Proponent's response to DFO comments on July 22, 2025. Changes are bolded below.*

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the replacement of the existing approach lights at the north and south end of the Erik Nielsen Whitehorse International Airport with a simplified short approach light system with runway alignment indicator lights. The Project will include the installation of 12 light towers at the north end and 12 light towers at the south end, a fence, and two new access roads. Project activities are primarily located on land owned and operated by the Government of Yukon- Highways and Public Works, with some activities located on land owned by the City of Whitehorse, a utility easement, and Crown Land. Activities are proposed to occur once permitting is obtained and will take up to 4 months to complete. The light system has a service life of 25 years and decommissioning activities are not anticipated.
The Project occurs within the Territories of Ta’an Kwäch’än Council and Kwanlin Dün First Nation. The Project is also located within the municipal boundary of the City of Whitehorse on lands zoned for airport service and overlaps with the greenbelt.

Other 
Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat

Mineral licks will not be disturbed or destroyed
If wildlife, such as moose or caribou, are encountered during operations, activities will be suspended until the wildlife has left the immediate area
If winter-road snow plowing occurs, a gap in the snowbanks will be created periodically to allow for unhindered wildlife travel
50-m buffers will be put in place upon any discovery of bat hibernacula or maternal roosts or any discovery of nests of raptors, early nesting birds, and shrub or ground nesting birds
Bear dens will not be damaged or disturbed; a 25-m buffer will be put around inactive dens, 100-m buffer around active individual black bear dens, and a 200-m buffer for a denning sow with cubs 
The creation of gaps in snowplowing created barriers (i.e. snowbanks) would occur in years of relatively high snowfall and could be as frequent as every 100 meters if deemed necessary/reasonable

YG Tourism and Heritage and the heritage departments of the First Nation of Na-cho Nyäk Dun and Selkirk First Nation will be contacted upon discovery of any suspected heritage sites or resources

All activities will follow the Forest Resources Regulation: Wetlands Riparian Management Standards and Guidelines
projectScope.summary: The Project is a salvage timber harvest operation in the Upland and Lowland Operating Units within the Draft 2025 Crooked Creek Timber Harvest area. The harvest area consists of three harvest blocks with a total area of 467 hectares. The project area, adjacent to the North Klondike Highway between km 513 and 521, is located approximately 13 km south of Stewart Crossing within the Territories of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun and Selkirk First Nation. The operating units will be accessed through the construction of new Class 4 Forest Resource Roads off the North Klondike Highway. Project activities will occur in all seasons when the ground is dry or frozen (to avoid rutting and compaction) and will continue for 10 years once permits are obtained. Silvicultural activities may occur for an additional 10 years based on regeneration assessments.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the development of a new timber harvest area, timber harvesting of fire-killed spruce and pine, and forest resource road development within the Scoby Creek area with the purpose of increasing the supply of fuel wood and saw logs available in southeast Yukon. The project area is located approximately 78 km east of Watson Lake and 57 km northeast of Lower Post, BC.  The commercial harvest of dead and downed timber and incidental harvest of green trees will occur within three operating units. Access to the project area is via the Coal River Road and existing roads. An additional 4.25 km of new forest resource roads are proposed to allow for access to harvest blocks. 
Project activities will be conducted year-round on dry or frozen ground, annually for 10 years. Multiple licenses may be active in the project area at any given time. Timber harvesting may occur in both summer and winter depending on ground conditions. The Project is within the Yukon portion of the Kaska Nation Traditional Territory (Liard First Nation and Ross River Dena Council as defined by YESAA).

projectScope.summary:
The Project is the construction, installation, and operation of a 375 m long single-phase power line to service Lot 232, Golden Horn Subdivision (2 Arctic Drive). The new powerline will connect to the existing line at the west end of Arctic Drive and will extend along the public Right-of-Way that is currently used as a driveway for Lot 177 and Lot 231, public trail, and river access. The Project is located on Crown Land within the boundaries of the Golden Horn Local Area Plan. The Project is within the territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta'an Kwäch'än Council.
Activities are anticipated to occur between January and April 2026 (contingent on ATCO completing their survey) and will take approximately two to three weeks to complete.

projectScope.summary: The Project is an amendment to an existing Class 3 placer mine (LP01475) on six claims, previously assessed by the Dawson Designated Office in 2022 (YESAB project 2022-0020). The amendment includes increasing the duration of the mining operation from five years to ten years, new trail development, and trenching. The Project is located along Mint Gulch in the Klondike River Watershed and the Territory of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in, approximately 35 km east of Dawson City. The Project falls within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. The Project will be accessed via the Hunker Creek Road. Activities are proposed from May 1 to October 5 annually until 2032.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 47 contiguous claims on Sulphur Creek, Friday Gulch, and an un-named left limit tributary (UNLLT) to Friday Gulch in the Indian River Watershed. The Project is within the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun Territories and falls within Land Management Unit 11 Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Land Use Plan. The Project is approximately 40 km southeast of Dawson City and will be accessed by the Goldfields road network, as well as new and existing on-claim access. Project activities are proposed to occur from April 1 to October 30, annually for 10 years.

Project will include two camps with a maximum of 25 people between two camps (15 full-time workers, and 10 part-time staff or visitors)
Camps will be closed at the end of the mining season. Perishable food items, garbage and special waste containers are removed from the site and disposed of in accordance with applicable regulations.

Non-Hazardous waste

Camp Garbage will be stored in secure steel bins, in Seacans, latched and locked

< 10 kg/day

Scrap metal

Progressive and Final Reclamation

Progressive reclamation program will continue from current operations and move upstream as mining cuts are no longer needed (2- 3 years). Final reclamation will happen in the final two years of the Project.

Coarse tailings and overburden stockpiles will be used to backfill and grade decommissioned mining cuts, reservoirs, and settling facilities. 
Fine sediments stockpiled during settling pond cleaning will be spread over the coarse tailings and overburden.
Intake and discharge ditches will also be backfilled. 

A permanent plug will be installed at the intake/discharge locations and armoured with washed tailings.

Stripped organics will then be placed over the fine materials and left to revegetate naturally.
Large trees, large boulders, and coarse gravel will be stored separately for reclamation and erosion control.
All equipment, structures, and waste will be removed
Tailings and other disturbed areas will be re-contoured to a 2:1 slope

Roads will be progressively reclaimed by scarifying the road surface.

Available organic materials will be spread over scarified roads in order to promote natural revegetation
Berms will be constructed at the head of roads and trails no longer required for placer or quartz mining/exploration in order to minimize further access. 

Out-of-stream reservoirs and/or settling ponds may be left as shallow open water ponds (< 1m in depth)

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 81 claims in the White River watershed. It includes two claim blocks: one with 74 contiguous claims along Summit Creek, Courtland Creek, and the upper reaches and tributaries of Nansen Creek, and another with 7 contiguous claims in the lower reaches of Nansen Creek. The Project is situated approximately 70 km west of the Village of Carmacks within the Territory of the Little Salmon Carmacks First Nation. Access will be via Mount Nansen Road and existing mine roads in the area. Activities are planned to occur annually from April 1 to November 15 over a period of up to 12 years.

projectScope.summary:
Government of Canada - Public Services and Procurement Canada - IAM, Alaska Highway is proposing the continued use and development of an existing 34.1 ha gravel quarry to supply gravel for use in maintenance, repair, and construction activities for the Alaska Highway. The estimated volume of aggregate resources at the site is 600 000 m3. The project area is located along the Alaska Highway, approximately 57.2 km east of the Town of Watson Lake and 35.6 km east of Lower Post, BC. The lot is accessed via an existing access road off at km 921.8 of the Alaska Highway.
Project activities may occur at any time during the year, depending on maintenance requirements and weather conditions. The expected life of the granular pit is over 100 years, but the temporal scope of this assessment is limited to project activities occurring during the initial 20 years post permitting.
The Project is located in the Territories of the Liard First Nation and Ross River Dena Council.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of placer mine on eight claims along Hunker Creek and 36 Pup, in the Klondike River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 40 km from Dawson City in the Traditional Territory of the Tr'ondek Hwech'in. The Project falls within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Dawson Planning Region {LMU 11) and will be accessed via Hunker Creek Road. Project activities will take place from May 15 to September 30, annually, for 10 years.

projectScope.summary:
The purpose of the Project is to harvest forest resources in the form of sawlogs, cabin logs, and fuelwood for home/industrial heating. The Project is located within the Dognose Operating Unit of the Draft 2024 Dempster Highway Timber Harvest Plan. Within the Dognose Operating Unit, four harvest blocks (126.3 hectares total) will be selectively harvested. The Project is located approximately 40 km east of Dawson City and is within the Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and in Land Management Unit 12: Tr’ondëk Täk’it (Klondike Valley) and Land Management Unit 6: Tr’ondëk (Klondike) of the recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan. Harvesting activities will span 10 years, followed by up to 10 years of silviculture activities, for a total temporal scope of up to 20 years. The project area will be accessed for harvesting via existing public and private roads, gated Forest Resource Roads and a new winter road will be required for harvest block access. The project area will be surveyed by remote sensing and helicopter to determine need for silviculture activities and accessed by helicopter, foot traverse, and all-terrain vehicle to complete silviculture activities. 

The scope was amended on March 11, 2025. Changes are in bold.

projectScope.summary: The Project is an amendment to an existing placer mine (PM24-013) previously assessed by the Designated Office in 2023 (YESAB Project 2023-0037). Proposed amendment activities are to facilitate the construction of a clear span bridge over Dominion Creek and will be limited to within one of the 48 claims previously assessed. The Project is located approximately 62 km south of Dawson City in the Traditional Territories of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of placer mining on 26 claims along California Creek and three of its tributaries. California Creek is a tributary of Sixty Mile River, in the Sixty Mile River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 45 km due west from Dawson City and will be accessed via an existing road network (e.g. Top of the World Highway and Sixty Mile Road). The Project is located within the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in Territory and falls within Land Management Unit 15: Khel Dëk (Sixty Mile) of the Recommended Dawson Land Use Plan. Project activities are scheduled to occur from April 1 to October 30, annually, for 10 years.

decisionBodies: YG EMR Land Management Branch, Client Services

projectScope.summary: The Project is the installation of two powerlines that will increase capacity and redundancy of power in the Mt. Lorne area. One powerline will be located at the intersection of Cowley Lake Road and the South Klondike Highway and the other at the intersection of Annie Lake Road and the South Klondike Highway, approximately 24 km and 32 km south of the City of Whitehorse, respectively. The Project is located within the Traditional Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Carcross/Tagish First Nation. Project activities are scheduled to occur within a two-week period during the month of October following permitting.

projectScope.summary: This Project is the reconstruction of the Silver Trail Highway between kilometres 0 and 57. The purpose of this project is to resolve safety, structural, drainage and geometric alignment issues. Work will generally be spot repairs to sections of the road. The community of Mayo is located at the northernmost extent of the project area and Pelly Crossing is located 62 km south of the project boundary. The Project is in the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun; no activities will occur on settlement land. Project activities will be 10 years in duration and are likely to commence in 2026.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is a placer mine on 21 claims along Scroggie Creek and multiple unnamed tributaries of Scroggie Creek in the Lower Stewart River Watershed. The project area is located approximately 110 km southeast of Dawson City and is proposed to be accessed by fixed wing aircraft using one existing and one proposed airstrip. The project area is within the Territories of Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and Selkirk First Nation and within the Asserted Territory of White River First Nation. Activities are proposed from April 1 to October 31 annually, for 10 years.
Approximately 18 m2 of Claim P 38970 overlaps with Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in settlement parcel TH S-209B1; however, the Proponent has indicated that a buffer will be established around the parcel and no activities will occur on settlement land.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation and operation of a placer mine on 12 claims along Larose Creek and Rabbit Creek in the Alsek River Watershed. projectScope.summary: The Project is for placer mining on 30 claims along Moose Creek, within the Yukon River Watershed in the Southern Lakes Region. The Project is located approximately 20 km southeast of Tagish and 18 km southwest of Jakes Corner, within Carcross/Tagish First Nation Traditional Territory. Project activities are proposed to take place from May 1 to October 31 annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The purpose of the project is to construct a total of approximately 2.0 km of new paved multi-use trail along the Alaska Highway between Two Mile Hill intersection and Kopper King neighbourhood and complete some highway modifications in the area between Two Mile Hill and the YG EMR building approximately 300 m to the north. The Project within the municipal boundaries of the City of Whitehorse and falls within the territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta’an Kwäch’än Council. Work will start in the fall of 2025, pause for the winter, and then recommence in the spring of 2026 and be completed by the fall of 2026.  To accommodate uncertainties, the temporal scope considered to complete trail construction activities is 10 years. Maintenance activities will be ongoing over 25 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continued operation of the existing Yukon Energy Corporation backup diesel-fired electricity generating facility. The Project is located within the City of Dawson Municipal Boundary and is located within the Territory of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in. The Project involves the continued operation of six diesel generators for the purpose of backup power generation during power outages or shortages. Total power production will be up to 7.1 MW of electricity per year for 20 consecutive years. Closure is not currently proposed for this permit, but removal of generators may occur if the end of lifespan is reached within the temporal scope.

The project involves excavating a paleoseismic trench to support earthquake hazard research, located about 40 km southeast of Dawson City within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Territory. Access to the Project will be via the Dempster Highway, approximately 200 m off the highway. Excavation and sampling of the proposed trench will occur during the summer season (August or September) of 2025 or 2026 and last approximately 2 weeks, with reclamation of the site taking an additional month.

projectScope.summary:
The Project is a quartz exploration program at the Blende Property on Mount Williams. The property consists of 260 Quartz claims. They may be accessed over the existing Silver Trail Highway and Mcquesten Lake Road, Wind River winter road or by helicopter from the Village of Mayo. The Project is located within the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun, approximately 65 km northeast of Keno. The Project is adjacent to Settlement Land parcel NND R-17A. No exploration activities will take place on Settlement Land. Project activities are proposed to occur from February 1 to September 15 for five years.
*Scope changes are in bold and are from IR 03 Response (YOR# 2025-0019-0146).

projectScope.summary: The Project is the establishment of camps along the North Alaska Highway in existing gravel pits, between km 1685 and 1902, to support maintenance and repair activities on the highway. The camps will also serve as staging areas for equipment, materials and fuel. The Project is located within the Territories of Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation. Six possible locations for camps are proposed, however only up to three camps will be active in any given year. The Project will take place over the next 15 years, predominantly between May and October; however, smaller camps that are self-contained could be established year-round to support granular production.

The Project is located approximately 34 km southeast of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Territories of the Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun. projectScope.activities:
Use of heavy equipment
Construction of a new access road (~200 x 7.5 m)
Conduct a Hydrogeological Assessment for water quality monitoring, including:

Drilling and installation of groundwater monitoring wells (1 upgradient and 3 downgradient)

Site preparation includes:

Vegetation clearing (~1500 m2 for the access road, 13 767.6 m2 for the site) 
Installation of perimeter fence (6 ft high) and gates
Construction of an earth berm to the west of the facility to separate the facility from the quarry for traffic control purposes and to keep surface water from entering the treatment area
Construction and use of lined treatment cells:

Maximum capacity of 15 000 m3 of contaminated soil treatment on 4 cells (for a maximum 104 m x 56 m):

Each cell will have a sump and be bermed and lined with either 30 mil or 60 mil synthetic liners; soils in cells will be max. ~26 m W x 56 m L x 4 m H; cell berm dimensions are 4 m W x 56 m L x 2 to 3 m H

Max. capacity 15 000 gal of contaminated water stored in 3 tanks of 5000 gal each (in a lined storage cell with a capacity of 253 m3)

Placement of a 12' x 16' mobile office on skids
Installation of a temporary contained privy during construction activities

Treatment procedures:

Treatment of contaminated soil using natural bioremediation (soil piles will not be turned on windy days and they will be kept moist for dust control)
Treatment of contaminated water by evaporation and sprayed onto the soil piles

Transportation of contaminated soil and water to the site via covered dump trucks and water trucks
Monitoring and sampling of all contaminated material being treated at the facility:

Biannual groundwater sampling and liner inspection as per the LTF permit requirements (aquatic life and drinking water standards will be followed)
Testing of sump water and soil before disposal
Employee on site regularly to conduct inspections (3 times per week and eventually daily)

Operational Commitments

No fuel stored on-site (refueling of equipment will 300 L Tidy Tanks)
Breaches or spills will be reported immediately, and an excavator or loader will be used to contain contaminated material, will follow the process for adaptive management outlined in the Yukon Contaminated Sites Regulations
Empty water storage tanks with pumps will be available on-site for contaminated water should flooding occur
Grading, sloping, drainage and erosion control measures implemented (will ensure clean surface water off-site will drain away from the facility 
Vehicle and equipment will be kept clean and free of mud/dirt/plant debris from tires and undercarriages

Decommissioning of the site (as per Environment Yukon Guidelines for Land Treatment Facilities):

Will sample and test ground to ensure there is no contamination
Will remove the liners from the site and dispose at an approved facility
Ground will be graded and contoured, and an organic layer will be placed over the disturbed area and seeded
Groundwater monitoring wells decommission as per Protocol No. 7
Submission of a report demonstrating that no contamination remains on site

Operational Considerations

The LTF will be designed and engineered by a third party engineer and will meet the applicable Canadian Dam Association standards for earthquakes and other environmental events
If contamination of nearby private wells or watercourses occurs as a result of the LTF, immediate and comprehensive remedial actions will be implemented by CRE to mitigate the impacts and restore water quality
An additional (third) groundwater monitoring well will be installed downgradient of the LTF to monitor for hydrocarbons
Castle Rock Enterprises (CRE) will ensure proper grading, sloping, drainage and erosion control measures are implemented at the  LTF
Soil in the LTF will not be disturbed during the winter months
The LTF will be inspected regularly during winter months
CRE will ensure the contaminated soil piles in the cells are kept moist to avoid contaminated soil being blown offsite
A water truck will be used on the access road during hauling to keep the dust levels to a minimum
Soil turning will not be conducted by CRE on windy days
A 3 m high berm will be built around the treatment facility to create a wind break
On top of that berm, on the north and south side of the facility, CRE will add an 8 ft high windbreak fence to further mitigate wind gusts
Geocloth will be added to the perimeter fence 
The orientation of the LTF will be east-west facing and the access road entrance will be moved to the eastern corner of the LTF boundary
The maximum biopile height will be reduced to 3 m to ensure the biopiles are not above the outside berm height
Berms will be 3 to 4 m wide by 56 m long and 2 to 3 m high; the berm to be built around the treatment facility will be 3 m high; each cell will have a sump located at the back of the cell, that will be 2 m deep and double lined
Sump water will be tested during the regular groundwater sampling events
Castle Rock's equipment will use GPS tracking technology to ensure the equipment does not make contact with the liner
A layer of sand will be placed on top of the cell liners, and Geocloth will be placed on top of the sand layer 
All CRE and subcontractor vehicles and equipment will be clean and free of mud/dirt/plant debris from tires and undercarriages
projectScope.summary: The Project is the construction, operation and decommissioning of a commercial Land Treatment Facility (LTF). The LTF will accept, store, and treat both highly and non-highly contaminated petroleum hydrocarbon impacted soil and water. The Project is located approximately 11 km west of Haines Junction, within the Traditional Territory of Champagne and Aishihik First Nations. The site is accessed through an existing access via the Mackintosh gravel pit and will require a new access road to be constructed through the existing gravel pit reserve; the proposed site will be located outside of the north boundary of the gravel pit reserve. Equipment will operate from 7 am to 7 pm. Clearing is planned in winter 2025 and construction between April and October 2025, or as soon as permitting is in place. Project activities are proposed to occur annually for 10 years, with the operational season from May to October.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the upgrading of an existing pull out at km 60 of the Top of the World Highway at the intersection with Clinton Creek Road. The Project is located approximately 60 km northwest of Dawson City. The upgraded pullout will be cleared and graded and will consist of a designated rest stop, picnic area and interpretive area. The Project is within TH Territory and is located within Land Management Unit 14: Tąy Dë̀kdhät (Top of the World) of the Dawson Planning Region. The Project will be completed over a maximum of 22 days of active work to occur between May 15 and October 15, 2026. Maintenance of the pullout will occur regularly after preparation to empty the privies and ensure safe access and use of the pull-out amenities.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the construction of a bike skills park within Dakwäkäda (Village of Haines Junction) in the area between Jackson Street, Alsek Crescent and Otter Crescent. It is located on Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Traditional Territory. The park will feature a looping trail system with rollers, banked turns, small jumps, wooden decks and obstacles. It will serve as a recreational area for community events, training sessions and social gatherings. The construction phase is planned to occur between June and October 2025 for 4 to 5 weeks. The use of the land is considered indefinite. However, the bike park is not a permanent infrastructure and can be reclaimed if needed.

projectScope.summary: The Project is an amendment of Air Emissions Permit No. 60-010-05 for the Mayo Secondary Thermal site to increase generating capacity from 4.9 MW to 9 MW (additional 4.1 MW capacity) using the existing five (5) diesel generators located on site. The amendment is proposed to address load growth as well as additional capacity required to account for lower reliability of the mobile rental generating units, scheduled asset retirements, and delays to other capacity projects. The Project is located at the existing Mayo Hydro Generating Station, 7 km North of the Village of Mayo, and within the Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. Project activities are proposed for 10 years (2025-2035).

projectScope.activities:
*Note* 
The Project Scope has been updated as a result of new information provided in the Response to Information Request 03 (see YOR 2025-0062-0072). These changes have been made in bold.
Throughout the Adequacy and Evaluation Stages, additional information/refined information about the Project was gained through Information Requests and through correspondence with the Proponent. While this information has been uploaded to the YOR, the Designated Office acknowledges that because of these modifications, there may be activities/quantities present within documents on the YOR that either have not been included in this section or have been varied. 
The list of activities presented below represents the most up-to-date understanding of the activities proposed for this Project. Additionally, this scope was reviewed by the Proponent.

Access

Air-based:

Fixed-wing aircraft to transport equipment, supplies, and personnel to the existing on claim Forks airstrip

Flights to originate from either the Mayo airport, the MacMillan Pass airstrip, Faro airport, or Ross River airstrip
Up to 1400 flights/ year
Existing Forks Airstrip to be extended by 200 m (total length approximately 1 115 m, 20 m wide)

Up to 100 flights/year in total on Arrowhead and Silent Lakes
Helicopter use to transport equipment, supplies, and personnel between Forks Airstrip and exploration areas

Up to 12 flights/day, up to 10 flight hours/day/helicopter
See YOR Document 2025-0062-0003 for further flight details 
The company will continue to follow the conditions of the sheep monitoring program developed by YG Environment and mandated by LQ00561. Helicopter flights to potentially occur over full claim block

Flying in Sheep Country and Flying in Caribou Country will be used as a guideline for all pilots and employees
Flight routes largely follow major valleys and avoid sensitive wildlife areas (summer sheep and caribou habitats, raptor nests, mineral licks)

If caribou are observed along main routes in high elevations or on ridge tops, an alternate lower elevation route followed
If ungulates or large carnivores are observed, flight routes will be modified to maximize the vertical and horizontal separation distance between the aircraft and the animals; whenever possible, a minimum horizontal distance of 1 000 m from ungulates or large carnivores will be maintained; where possible, flight routes will place a mountain ridge between aircraft and animals until they have left the area
If an evasion response is detected, aircraft will ascend to higher flight altitude and veer away from animals when safe
Flight routes will be adjusted to avoid areas where animals were observed

The Proponent will communicate with other users in the area and work to reduce the number and frequency of flights in the area to the extent possible

Ground-based, on-claims:

Construction of new trails: up to 25 km x 4 m
Upgrading of existing trails to roads: up to 1.8 km x 6 m
Construction of new roads: up to 13.2 km x 6 m

Vegetative mat removed and stockpiled for later reclamation, benching on steeper slopes


Winter trails, on-claims:

Up to 40 km between Arrowhead Lake and the valley target

Up to 5 m wide 
Base 10 cm packed snow
Used to mobilize heavy equipment/supplies
Up to 10 trips over four seasons

Two route options (see YOR Document 2025-0062-0039), final route chosen based on avalanche risk and terrain 
Crossing of Rogue River may be required depending on route chosen; will follow Fisheries and Oceans Canada (DFO) "Code of practice: Ice bridges and snow fills".
Winter trail will not connect to existing road networks
Winter trail utilized into late winter as conditions permit, 
Constructed by vegetation clearing, vegetative mat left intact, snow compacted and/or plowed, gaps in snowbanks left every 300 m in areas where ungulates are known to occur

Reclamation of all roads and trails includes recontouring, scarifying compacted surfaces, replacing stockpiled vegetative mat where removed

Monitoring of revegetation success (80% vegetation cover relative to adjacent areas)

If natural revegetation is unsuccessful, active seeding with native species in third summer

Construction of up to 2 clear-span bridge crossings (25 m long x 5 m wide)

Bridges constructed between June and September
The Proponent will hire an engineer to identify the optimal locations for bridges and design them accordingly and adhere to DFO’s “Code of Practice: Clear Span Bridges” for construction and use
Bridges will be reclaimed following the “Yukon Chamber of Mines: Mineral Exploration Best Management Practices and Regulatory Guide”
Locations to be finalized by exploration

Construction of up to 6 culverts

Locations to be finalized by exploration

Up to 50 km x 1 m

Up to 10 km x 1 m

Both cut lines and corridors will be cut using hand-held tools and vegetative mat will remain

Drilling (Diamond, Sonic, RC, and RAB)

Will primarily use diamond drilling, may use sonic, reverse circulation, or rotary air blast
Use of bentonite and/or biodegradable mud

Drill equipment transported by helicopter
Drilling may occur within 30 m of watercourse, as far away as practicable

Erosion and sedimentation controls (e.g. silt fences, straw waters) for drill water and cuttings installed between drilling activities and watercourse
No discharging of any material to watercourse
All works within 30 m of watercourse will not proceed without prior approval from DFO
Disturbed footprint and vegetation removal will be minimized to the smallest extent possible

Sumps (up to 3 m x 2 m x 1 m)

Sumps flagged with survey ribbon or snow fence
Personnel will be present 24 hours a day

Core stored in covered racks or on pallets with lids

Up to 240 m3/day for drilling, sourced from nearby watercourses
Up to 30 m3/day for each camp use, sourced from nearby watercourses (Marmot Creek at Forks Camp, unnamed creek at Valley Camp), filtered and treated by UV, and stored in tanks

Installation and use of up to 2 water wells for drinking water, 1 at Forks Camps and 1 at Valley Camp

Intakes will be fitted with fish screens according to DFO guidelines

Groundwater Wells

Up to 95 groundwater monitoring wells installed throughout project area:

Up to 45 standpipe monitoring wells, 5.8 cm (2”) diameter, 25 m deep
Up to 10 pumping wells, 10.2 cm (4”) diameter, 25 m deep
Up to 20 Vibrating Wire Peizometers 

Wells sealed and disturbed areas graded
To remain for full duration of project

Camps and Waste Management

Use of 2 existing camps

Up to 50 persons at each, up to 100 persons total 
Up to 12 500 person-days each, up to 25 000 person-days total
Person-days concentrated between May and October

Located on-claims, over 30 m from the nearest watercourse

Forks Camp located on grants YE51996 and YE51994, existing disturbance footprint is 14 000 m2

An additional 10 000 m2 clearing for each camp, 20 000 m2 of clearing total
Camps consist of frame/log structures and canvas tents

Frame/log structures dismantled, and timber burned on site at final reclamation

Seasonal shut-down includes removal of canvas tents, storage of materials within closed office or kitchen buildings 
Human waste disposed of in pit privies (6 at each camp), septic field at each camp may be built
Greywater disposed of in a sump

2 sumps/camp

Forks Camp: 1 x 2 x 1 m; 3 x 3 x 1 m
Valley Camp: 3 x 3 x 1.5 m

Backfilled seasonally if no longer needed for winter operations

Camp and kitchen waste incinerated daily onsite (3 m3/day/camp) in Fire Lake A600 incinerator, ashes buried onsite or flown out (4 m3/month/camp)
Non-combustible and hazardous waste stored onsite in secure containers within bermed area, removed from site during regular trips and disposed of at appropriate facilities, storage time lengths variable and dependant on use of materials
At seasonal shut-down, remaining petroleum products secured, covered, stored in lined-berm and/or double-walled tanks onsite, all other waste removed 
Electric fence surrounding camps
All employees trained in bear safety and waste management protocols, bear deterrents available on-site

Fuel Use and Storage

Up to 60 000 L diesel; 60 000 L jet A; 4 200 L gasoline; 5 000 lbs propane

Total fuel amounts to be dispersed across 2 camps

All fuel stored at camps within secondary containment consisting of lined berm with 110% capacity, located over 100 m from nearest watercourse

Diesel, jet A fuel, gasoline stored in either 205 L drums or 500 or 900 L transfer tanks
Diesel and jet A fuel stored in 22 000 L bladders or double-walled bulk tanks
Propane stored in 100 or 420 lb tanks

Fuel transported to Forks Airstrip in bladders and pumped directly into onsite storage bladders or by fixed wing aircraft in 205 L drums
Fuel will be transported from Forks Airstrip to Valley Camp by helicopter in transfer tanks
Fuel will be transported to drill sites by helicopter in transfer tanks or through vehicles
Fuel transported to exploration sites in drums and/or tanks and stored in a lined berm area with 100% containment, over 30 m from nearest watercourse
Water pumps may be refuelled up to 5 m from watercourse, fuel to be stored at least 7 m from watercourse within secondary containment of at least 110% capacity or within double-walled tidy tanks
Spill Contingency Plan in place 
Spill kits on-site and with all pieces of heavy equipment

Use of Heavy Equipment
Progressive and Final Reclamation

Seasonal closure/ progressive reclamation:

Disturbed areas with no vegetative mat removal (cut lines, corridors and helicopter pads) left for natural revegetation
Disturbed areas with vegetative mat removal (clearings, roads, and trails) recontoured, compacted surfaces scarified if needed, and stockpiled vegetative mat replaced progressively
Disturbed areas with steep slopes will be recontoured to a 2:1 slope

If a 2:1 slope cannot be achieved, diversion swales or ditches will be placed around the crest of the slope to reduce surface runoff on the slope face
Other erosion and sedimentation controls may be used, including surface covers, revegetation, and benching

Excavations (drillholes, trenches and sumps) backfilled, organics and vegetative mat replaced, recontoured progressively

Drillholes to remain for up to 2 days before being capped/plugged 
Trenches to remain for up to 2 weeks before being backfilled and reclaimed
Sumps backfilled with organics and replaced on top when drilling is complete

Surface slopes recontoured, vegetative mat and soils stockpiled separately, replaced during progressive reclamation at the end of each drilling season

Final decommissioning and reclamation

All remaining disturbed areas (clearings, roads, trails) will be reclaimed as described above 
Ditches or berms regraded
On steep slopes, berms will be built to divert surface runoff on the slope face
All timber structures will be dismantled 
Status of roads, trails, drill sites, and ongoing/completed reclamation reported annually


Settlement Land

Project surrounds, but does not overlap with, Settlement Land parcels NND S-186B1 and NND S-114B1
No work to occur on these parcels

General Wildlife Mitigations

If ungulates or large carnivores are encountered on active work areas, all work activities will be suspended until the animals have left the area on their own accord
All discovered raptor nests, mineral licks, bear dens, or sharp-tailed grouse leks will be reported to the Wildlife Key Area Program and the Proponent will discuss potential mitigations to protect these features with the program staff
See YOR Document 2025-0062-0074 for further details regarding wildlife mitigations for thinhorn sheep, caribou, moose, bears, avian wildlife, wolverine, collared pika, Yukon Conservation Data Reportable Species

General Soils and Vegetation Mitigations

Staff will familiarize themselves with invasive species identification and methods to reduce introduction and management of established populations
All vehicles will be clean and free of foreign soil and plant material
All activities will follow recommendations in Preferred practices for works affecting Yukon waters document

Personal and Community Safety

Staff primarily travel from Whitehorse to the project area via air transit

If travel through Mayo, Faro, Ross River is required, all staff and contractors must follow Proponent’s Standards of Conduct in Communities 
All staff receive cultural awareness training, includes information on First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun Territory, cultural awareness, and Proponent’s commitment to respect and safety on the land


Heritage Resources

If a suspected heritage resource is encountered, personnel will stop work, flag area, avoid works within 30 m of the location, and notify Yukon Archeology Program and Na-Cho Nyäk Dun First Nation
Archaeological Chance Find Procedure in place
projectScope.summary: The Project is a quartz mineral exploration program on the Rogue Properties, which consists of 5 794 claims. The Project is located within the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun Territory and is directly adjacent to and surrounds two Settlement Land parcels (NND S-186B1 and NND S-114B1). It is approximately 200 km east of the Village of Mayo and 160 northeast of the community of Faro. Access to the project area will primarily be by fixed-wing aircraft landing at the Forks Airstrip and secondly by helicopter to the Forks Airstrip or various other claim blocks. Within the claim area, access will be via helicopter as well as existing and newly constructed on-claim roads, trails, and winter trails. There are two existing on-claim camps, housing approximately 50 persons at each, that will be used. Activities will occur year-round, annually for 10 years.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a placer mine on 61 claims along the two forks of Shanghai Creek and its pups. Shanghai Creek is part of the Et'o Nyäk Tagé (McQuesten River), in the Yukon River Watershed. The Project is located approximately 6 km northwest of the community of Elsa and 40 km southwest of the Village of Mayo in the Traditional Territory of the Na-Cho Nyäk Dun First Nation Traditional Territory. The Project will be accessed via Hansen McQuesten Road and West McQuesten Road, and subsequently by an assessed and permitted road by GroundTruth Exploration leading into the claims (YOR # 2020-0078 and Permit # LQ00544). Project activities will occur annually from April 15 to October 31 for ten years.

projectScope.summary:
Scope amended to correct Placer number P522281 from P522218 May 14/24
The Project is the amendment to an existing placer mining operation (Permit PM17-050), previously assessed by the Dawson Designated Office in 2017 (YOR #2017-0005), located on the Sixty Mile River and UNRLT 5 within the Sixty Mile River watershed. Amended activities are only applicable to 6 additional claims (P530160, P530159, P530158, P522281, P522282, P522283) and 3 additional leases (ID02120, ID02121, ID02122). No new activities are being proposed on existing claims. The amendment includes additional overburden removal, sluicing and water usage. Exploration drilling, creation of in-steam dugouts, out-of-stream settling facilities, settling ponds are also proposed. Development of new roads and trails is planned but was included in the previous assessment. Camp, fuel storage, waste management previously assessed are not included in this assessment.
The Project is located approximately 50 km west of Dawson City within the Traditional Territory of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in First Nation and within Land Management Unit 15: Khel Dëk (Sixty Mile) of the Dawson Planning Region. Project will be accessed using the existing Top of The World Highway down from Swede Dome to the top end of California Creek or from km 88 down through Hawk Mining to the top end of the claims on Sixty Mile River. Project activities are proposed to occur from May 1 to October 31, annually until 2027.

projectScope.summary: The Project is the continuation of a placer mine on 19 claims along the Unnamed Left Limit Tributaries (UNLLT) and left limit bench of Sulphur Creek in the Indian River watershed. The Project is located approximately 55 km northwest of Dawson City, within Land Management Unit 11: Goldfields of the Recommended Dawson Regional Land Use Plan and within the Territories of Tr’ondëk Hwëch’in and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. Access to the Project will be via the North Klondike Highway and the existing Goldfields road network. Project activities will occur between March 15 and October 31, annually, for up to 10 years.

projectScope.activities:
Timing

Project activities may occur throughout the year.
From October 21 to April 15, all activities within the critical winter range of woodland caribou habitat areas will be suspended
Pause of surface disturbing work during spring thaw conditions (April-May)

Access

Use and maintenance of up to 30 km of existing roads
Upgrades of existing on-claim roads (up to 3 km x 5 m)
Use of 5km of existing trails
Construction of new roads (up to 5 km x 5 m) on claims
Construction of new trails (up to 20 km x 5 m) on claims
Construction of temporary trails (up to 10 km x 5 m) on claims
New roads and trails will only be constructed within the four target areas
Line cutting using hand tools (up to 35 km x 1.5 m)
No use of helicopter, fixed-wing aircraft, or drones in project area
Water Crossing

Use of proposed bridge or culvert across Cowley Creek
Use of up to 1 ford across Cowley Creek
Use of water crossing up to four times per day

No construction of corridors
Trails will be avoided where possible.
Where trails cannot be avoided, activities will be scheduled to minimize disruption, safe passage will be maintained for all users during operations, rubber-tracked equipment will be used, and any temporary impacts will be promptly reclaimed to pre-activity conditions. 
Advance notice of planned work will be communicated to organized trail user groups and land managers.
No use of residential neighbourhood roads to access project area

Clearing (drill pads)

Up to 400 clearings total

Up to 80 000 m2 of vegetative mat cleared in total
Up to 200 m2 per clearing of vegetative mat removed
Up to 150 m2 per clearing of vegetative mat intact (only trees and brush removed)
No clearing within 30 metres of a watercourse
Vegetative mat will be stockpiled for reclamation
Clearings will be progressively reclaimed


Exploration

Use of heavy equipment

Equipment transported onto and between sites by flat-deck trailer, tractor-trailer, track mounted drill and/or bulldozer pulling skid-mounted drills and sampling equipment

Drilling

Diamond drilling: up to 480 holes for up to 120 000 m total length
Reverse circulation: up to 120 holes for up to 30 000 m in total length
Use of up to 4 diamond drill rigs at any one time across the four target work areas, with up to 2 drills operating per target work area and at any given time
Drilling will occur 800 m or more from sensitive residential areas and sensitive receptors. Sensitive receptors include occupied structures outside of mapped residential areas
Drill cores transported off site daily
No drill sites within 30 m of a watercourse
Drillhole collars will be cemented and cut flush to ground as part of reclamation

Construction and use of sumps (up to 2 x 2 x 2 m) at each drill site for drill fluids and cuttings

Use of biodegradable drilling additives (bentonite clay or synthetic)
Sumps will be sloped on the uphill side to allow egress by wildlife
Sumps will be reclaimed immediately after use
Sumps will be backfilled and topped with a vegetative mat as part of reclamation

Rock and Soil Sampling
Geophysical Surveys

Water Use

Up to 160 m3 per day per watershed

Up to 80 m3 per day per drill
Water is withdrawn from watercourses within each work area or trucked from off-site sources, including McIntyre Creek, Heather Lake, Little Chief Pit, Middle Chief Lake, Big Chief Lake, Gem Pit, Black Cub South Pit and Cowley Creek

Water is transferred to the drill by pump and hose
No water returned to any watercourse
Use of centrifuge to recycle water

Wetlands

No ground disturbing activities within 30 m of a wetland
Verification of wetlands prior to project activities through the following:

Desktop screening
Field verification
Operational controls
Monitoring and documentation


Fuel Use & Storage

Mobile storage and transport of up to 800 L of diesel
Diesel stored in mobile truck-mounted slip tanks (200 L/ tank) with secondary containment
Fuel transferred directly to equipment from truck-mounted slip tanks
Spill kits present at work sites, trucks, and pumps
Fuel transfer will occur at or greater than 30 m from the highwater mark of any watercourse
Waste petroleum products will be stored in appropriate containers and promptly removed to an approved waste disposal facility
Slip tanks will be filled at licensed commercial fueling stations within the City of Whitehorse

Baseline Environmental Data Collection

Noise monitoring
Water quality and flow monitoring
Wildlife monitoring

Waste Management

Waste placed in temporary receptacles at each work site
Portable toilets will be used on site
Waste will be stored in bear-proof containers and hauled off-site for disposal daily
All garbage and hazardous waste will be disposed of at the appropriate facility
No waste material will be deposited or disposed of within 30 m of a watercourse

Reclamation

Reclamation of newly disturbed areas on an ongoing basis

Removed vegetation will be segregated into vegetative mat and overburden piles
Rock and soil will be replaced and vegetative mat returned
New drill sites recontoured to pre-existing slope
New drill sites will be cleaned of all materials and refuse will be removed from site
New access routes will be fully reclaimed by slope recontouring and replacement of vegetative mat

Reclamation will occur prior to expiry of authorization
Oversight of reclamation through site supervision, documented inspections and photographic records.
During excavation, material will be carefully segregated into vegetative mat and overburden/bedrock stockpiles

Other

Gladiator’s site supervisor will coordinate daily activity schedules with WMF staff and implement any monitoring requirements outlined by the City of Whitehorse.
Gladiator will provide the City of Whitehorse a minimum of 30 days’ notice prior to the start of onsite work.
No disturbance or displacement of known surface or subsurface infrastructure.

Heritage Resources

No land altering activities within an Area of Elevated Potential for Heritage Resources or within 30 metres of a Heritage Site.
Engage early and continuously with local First Nations, community groups, and stakeholders to identify and protect areas of cultural significance
Notification to relevant First Nations upon discovery of a Heritage Resource
No work proposed on KDFN or TKC settlement lands

Noise

Acoustic monitoring
Use of sound-dampening tools

Insulated drill shacks, use of rubber (rather than metal) mallets for drilling and maintenance

Heavy equipment use is restricted between 11 p.m. and 7 a.m. unless necessary for safety or environmental reasons.
Use of tracked equipment reduces noise impacts
Conduct noise checks in response to any noise-related concern or request from nearby residents or rights-holders.
Acoustics monitoring is available to select residents located within approximately 1 km of the active work areas.

Dust

Monitoring of conditions by field personnel during dry summer conditions
If determined to be warranted, implementation of suppression measures:

Limiting vehicle movement, reducing speed limits on dirt roads to a maximum of 30 km/hr on project access routes where permitted, and targeted dust-suppressant as needed.


Community Notifications

Broader project updates and planned field programs will be shared publicly

Information will be shared through regular technical press releases, community newsletters, social media, city council presentations, and community open houses (advertised on social media and in local newspapers).


Organized User Groups

Advance notice to representatives of activities that could affect access

Project Oversight

Employment of full-time field personnel responsible for overseeing and upholding protocols and procedures.
Regular review of company procedures based on site conditions, regulatory feedback and community input.
Application of internal compliance measures, including:

Pre-, during-, and post-drill site documentation;
Regular drill pad and water source inspections during drilling;
Wildlife sighting documentation from employees and contractors;
Internal flora, fauna, and noise monitoring surveys;
Onsite marking and monitoring of drill pad boundaries to limit cleared areas; and
Immediate corrective actions if non-conformances are identified.


Chasàn Chùa Territorial Park

No ground disturbing activities within the park boundary

Wildlife

Wildlife Key Areas, mapped wetlands, waterbodies, and watercourses will be reviewed in advance to guide site selection and access routes.
Prior to new ground disturbance, site supervisors will check for active nests or nesting activity and will adjust or delay work in that location, if necessary
Work plans will be adjusted to minimize conflicts with observed and nesting species in the Project area
Method for identifying bird species at risk and habitat:

Desktop screening
Field verification
Operational Controls
Monitoring and Documentation

No land clearing within 1 kilometer of known or observed raptor nests during the raptor nesting period, from May 1 to August 31
Any observed raptor nest sites will be reported to Environment Yukon’s Conservation Data Centre.
All migratory bird sightings and encounters will be immediately reported to the supervisor and recorded in the company Wildlife Log.
All activities will be suspended during the winter months, from October 21 to April 15, within identified woodland caribou habitat areas in an effort to avoid disturbing animals in their critical winter range.
If caribou are observed within 1 kilometer of active work areas, operations will halt until the animals move off on their own.
projectScope.summary: The Project is a quartz exploration program on 159 mineral claims and 6 Crown Grants in four target areas that will be accessed by road including the Copper Haul Road, McLean Lake Road, and Mount Sima Road. The Project is located within the territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta'an Kwäch'än Council. Project activities will occur both within and outside of the City of Whitehorse municipal boundary. The Project expands on work completed by Gladiator Metals Corp. under Class 1 notifications. Project activities outside of the four main target areas will exclude drilling, land clearing, construction of access roads and trails. The Proponent may carry out prospecting, mapping, sampling, and ground geophysical surveys outside of the four main target areas. Due to the location of the Project within the City of Whitehorse, on-site accommodations and structures are not part of the Project. Project activities will occur year-round for 5 years, with drill programs expected to occur for 30 to 150 days per year.

The Project is the clearing of up to 1 050 m2 of vegetation to facilitate the connection of an existing overhead powerline to future electrical infrastructure on Lot 1-077, Group 757 west of Watson Lake. The Project is located in the Territories of the Liard First Nation and the Ross River Dena Council. Project activities will require up to four weeks to complete and are proposed to start May/June 2026. 
No activities on private property are proposed.
